parts.content: State the type of bonding in sodium chloride.
parts.markscheme:
- Ionic bonding <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the question text
steps.content: To answer this question, we first need to look at the information provided in the introductory sentence. This question falls under **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** in your IB Chemistry syllabus, which explores how atoms form compounds through the transfer of electrons.

steps.title: Identify the classification
steps.content:
The question specification explicitly tells us how to classify the substance: 

"Sodium chloride is an example of an **ionic compound**."

steps.title: Determine the bonding type
steps.content: By definition, an ionic compound is a substance held together by the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions. Therefore, if the substance is an ionic compound, the type of bonding present is **ionic bonding**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The type of bonding in sodium chloride is:

**Ionic bonding**

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type, we first identify the elements present in the compound. Sodium chloride is composed of two elements: sodium ($Na$) and chlorine ($Cl$).

You can find these elements on the Periodic Table, which is the foundation of **Topic S3.1: The periodic table: Classification of elements**.

steps.title: Classify the sodium
steps.content: Sodium ($Na$) is located in Group 1 on the left side of the periodic table. Elements in this region are classified as **reactive metals** because they easily lose their single valence electron to achieve a stable configuration.

steps.title: Classify the chlorine
steps.content: Chlorine ($Cl$) is located in Group 17 on the right side of the periodic table. Elements in this group are classified as **non-metals**. They are highly electronegative and have a strong tendency to gain electrons.

steps.title: Determine bonding type
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, a classic rule of thumb is that bonding between a **metal** and a **non-metal** typically involves the transfer of electrons. 

When sodium (a metal) transfers an electron to chlorine (a non-metal), they form $Na^{+}$ and $Cl^{-}$ ions. The electrostatic attraction between these oppositely charged ions is what we call an **ionic bond**. This concept is detailed in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Based on the classification of sodium as a metal and chlorine as a non-metal, the bonding in sodium chloride is **ionic**.

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type using the electronegativity approach, we first identify the two elements in the compound. Sodium chloride consists of the metal **sodium (Na)** and the non-metal **chlorine (Cl)**.

This falls under **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** and involves understanding the continuum of bonding described in **Topic S2.4: From models to materials**.

steps.title: Find electronegativity values
steps.content:
Next, we look up the Pauling electronegativity values ($̇\chi$) for these elements in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet (typically found in the table for Periodicity). 

$$\chi(\text{Na}) = 0.9$$
$$\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$$

steps.title: Calculate the difference
steps.content:
We calculate the electronegativity difference ($Δ\chi$) between the two atoms. This difference tells us how equally (or unequally) the electrons are shared.

$$\Delta\chi = |\chi(\text{Cl}) - \chi(\text{Na})|$$
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 0.9 = 2.3$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the subtraction on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference between Chlorine and Sodium.

steps.title: Classify the bonding
steps.content:
According to the IB syllabus (specifically **Topic S2.4.1: Bonding as a continuum**), a bonding type is considered predominantly **ionic** if the electronegativity difference is greater than approximately $1.8$.

Since our calculated value of $2.3$ is significantly higher than $1.8$, the bond is classified as ionic.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The type of bonding in sodium chloride is **ionic bonding**.

This is because there is a large difference in electronegativity, leading to the complete transfer of an electron from sodium to chlorine, forming $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions.

parts.content: Identify the ions formed when sodium reacts with chlorine.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{Na}$ loses one electron to form $\text{Na}^+$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\text{Cl}$ gains one electron to form $\text{Cl}^-$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze sodium's configuration
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) has the electronic configuration **(2, 8, 1)**. It has one valence electron in its outermost shell. To achieve a stable noble gas configuration (a full outer shell), it is energetically favorable for sodium to lose this single electron. 

When a neutral atom loses one negatively charged electron, it becomes a positive ion with a $+1$ charge:

$$Na \rightarrow Na^{+} + e^{-}$$

steps.title: Analyze chlorine's configuration
steps.content:
Chlorine ($Cl$) has the electronic configuration **(2, 8, 7)**. It has seven valence electrons, meaning it only needs one more electron to complete its outer shell and achieve a stable octet configuration (2, 8, 8).

When a neutral atom gains one negatively charged electron, it becomes a negative ion with a $-1$ charge:

$$Cl + e^{-} \rightarrow Cl^{-}$$

steps.title: Identify the ions
steps.content:
By comparing the requirements for stability, we can identify the two ions formed during the reaction. Sodium becomes the sodium cation, and chlorine becomes the chloride anion. 

This process is the basis of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** in your syllabus.

The final ions are:
- **$Na^{+}$** (Sodium ion)
- **$Cl^{-}$** (Chloride ion)

steps.title: Locate sodium in Group 1
steps.content:
First, we look at the Periodic Table to find sodium ($\text{Na}$). Sodium is an alkali metal located in **Group 1**. 

Elements in Group 1 have one valence electron. To achieve a stable noble gas electron configuration (a full outer shell), they tend to lose this single electron.

steps.title: Determine the sodium ion
steps.content:
When a neutral sodium atom loses one negatively charged electron, it is left with one more proton than it has electrons. This results in a positive charge of $+1$.

$$\text{Na} \rightarrow \text{Na}^{+} + e^{-}$$

steps.title: Locate chlorine in Group 17
steps.content:
Next, we find chlorine ($\text{Cl}$) on the Periodic Table. It is a halogen located in **Group 17**. 

Elements in Group 17 have seven valence electrons. To achieve a stable noble gas configuration, they need to gain one more electron to complete their outer shell.

steps.title: Determine the chloride ion
steps.content:
When a neutral chlorine atom gains one negatively charged electron, it then has one more electron than it has protons. This results in a negative charge of $-1$.

$$\text{Cl} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^{-}$$

steps.title: State the final ions
steps.content:
By identifying their positions in the Periodic Table, we can conclude that the reaction involves the transfer of one electron from sodium to chlorine.

The ions formed are:
- Sodium ion: $\text{Na}^{+}$
- Chloride ion: $\text{Cl}^{-}$

steps.title: Determine valence electrons
steps.content:
To visualize the reaction, we first look at the periodic table (or recall Topic S1.3) to find the number of valence electrons for each atom:

*   **Sodium (Na)** is in Group 1, so it has **1** valence electron.
*   **Chlorine (Cl)** is in Group 17, so it has **7** valence electrons.

This is the starting point for our Lewis dot-and-cross diagram.

steps.title: Visualize the electron transfer
steps.content:
In an ionic reaction, atoms move electrons to achieve a stable full outer shell (an octet). 

Imagine the sodium atom with one dot (its electron) and the chlorine atom with seven crosses (its electrons). Sodium "gives" its one electron to chlorine so that both can reach a stable state.

$$\text{Na}\cdot + :\underset{\text{x x}}{\overset{\text{x x}}{\text{Cl}}} \text{x} \rightarrow [\text{Na}]^+ + [:\underset{\text{x x}}{\overset{\text{x x}}{\text{Cl}}}\cdot \text{x} ]^-$$

steps.title: Identify the sodium ion
steps.content:
When the sodium atom loses its one valence electron, it has more protons than electrons. This results in a positive charge.

As the markscheme notes, **Na loses one electron to form $\text{Na}^+$**. This is the sodium ion.

steps.title: Identify the chloride ion
steps.content:
The chlorine atom gains that one electron from sodium. Since it now has one extra negative electron compared to its protons, it takes on a negative charge.

As the markscheme notes, **Cl gains one electron to form $\text{Cl}^-$**. This is the chloride ion.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The two ions formed in the reaction are:

*   Sodium ion: **$\text{Na}^+$**
*   Chloride ion: **$\text{Cl}^-$**

parts.content: Describe the electrostatic interaction that holds the lattice together.
parts.markscheme:
- Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions <marks>A1</marks>
- Arranged in a regular 3D lattice structure <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the bonding force
steps.content:
The interaction holding sodium chloride together is the **strong electrostatic attraction** between **oppositely charged ions**. 

In the case of $NaCl$, these are the positive sodium ions ($Na^+$) and the negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$). This attraction acts in all directions around each ion.

highlights.text: Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions

steps.title: Describe the lattice structure
steps.content:
Rather than existing as individual molecules, these ions are arranged in a **regular 3D lattice structure**. 

This predictable, repeating pattern ensures that the attractive forces between opposite charges are maximized while the repulsive forces between like charges are minimized, resulting in a very stable crystal.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks for this question, your description should include:
1. The nature of the force: **Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions**.
2. The arrangement: A **regular 3D lattice**.

parts.content: Write the electron configurations of magnesium and oxide ions.
parts.markscheme:
- Magnesium ion, $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$: $\mathrm{1s^2 2s^2 2p^6}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxide ion, $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$: $\mathrm{1s^2 2s^2 2p^6}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Neutral Magnesium configuration
steps.content:
To find the configuration of the magnesium ion, we first look at a neutral magnesium ($\text{Mg}$) atom. According to the periodic table, magnesium has an atomic number of 12, meaning it has 12 electrons.

Following the Aufbau principle and orbital rules (from **Topic S1.3**), we fill the sublevels in order:

$$\text{Mg: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2$$

steps.title: Derive the Magnesium ion
steps.content:
The question asks for the magnesium ion, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$. The $2+$ charge indicates that the neutral atom has lost two electrons.

We remove these two electrons from the highest energy level (the $3s$ orbital):

$$\text{Mg}^{2+}: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

This leaves the ion with a stable octet, matching the noble gas configuration of Neon.

steps.title: Neutral Oxygen configuration
steps.content:
Next, we look at a neutral oxygen ($\text{O}$) atom. Oxygen has an atomic number of 8, so it has 8 electrons in its neutral state.

Following the filling rules:

$$\text{O: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^4$$

steps.title: Derive the Oxide ion
steps.content:
The oxide ion is $\text{O}^{2-}$. The $2-$ charge indicates that the neutral oxygen atom has gained two electrons.

We add these two electrons to the outermost $2p$ sublevel, which has room for them:

$$\text{O}^{2-}: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

Both ions are now **isoelectronic**, meaning they have the same electron configuration ($1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$).

methods.label: Total Electron Count and Aufbau Principle

steps.title: Identify neutral atomic numbers
steps.content:
To find the electron configuration of ions, we first look at the atomic numbers ($Z$) of the neutral elements from the periodic table. 

- Magnesium ($\mathrm{Mg}$) has $Z = 12$.
- Oxygen ($\mathrm{O}$) has $Z = 8$.

These numbers tell us how many protons (and therefore how many electrons) are in the neutral atoms. This is the foundation for finding the configuration in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Calculate total ion electrons
steps.content:
Next, we adjust the electron count based on the charge of the ions:

- For $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$, the $+2$ charge means it has **lost 2 electrons**.
  $$\text{Total electrons} = 12 - 2 = 10$$
- For $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$, the $-2$ charge means it has **gained 2 electrons**.
  $$\text{Total electrons} = 8 + 2 = 10$$

Both ions have 10 electrons, which means they are **isoelectronic**.

steps.title: Apply the Aufbau Principle
steps.content:
Now we fill the atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy according to the **Aufbau Principle**. We fill them until we reach our total of 10 electrons:

1.  **1s orbital**: Holds up to 2 electrons $\rightarrow \mathrm{1s^2}$ (8 remaining)
2.  **2s orbital**: Holds up to 2 electrons $\rightarrow \mathrm{2s^2}$ (6 remaining)
3.  **2p orbital**: Holds up to 6 electrons $\rightarrow \mathrm{2p^6}$ (0 remaining)

Following **S1.3.5 Orbital configurations and rules**, we sum these up to get the full configuration.

steps.title: State the final configurations
steps.content:
Since both ions have exactly 10 electrons, they share the same electron configuration. Both have reached the stable octet of the noble gas neon ($[\mathrm{Ne}]$).

- Magnesium ion ($\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$): $\mathrm{1s^2 2s^2 2p^6}$
- Oxide ion ($\mathrm{O}^{2-}$): $\mathrm{1s^2 2s^2 2p^6}$

parts.content: Explain how magnesium forms an ion and why it achieves a noble gas configuration.
parts.markscheme:
- Magnesium loses two electrons from outer shell to form $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Achieves the stable electron configuration of neon (a noble gas)
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content: Magnesium ($Mg$) is located in **Group 2** of the periodic table. According to the valence shell approach, the group number tells us that a neutral magnesium atom has **two electrons** in its outermost (valence) shell. To better understand these patterns, you can refer to **Structure 3.1: The periodic table**.

steps.title: Explain ion formation
steps.content:
To achieve a stable arrangement, magnesium undergoes a process called ionization. It **loses its two valence electrons** from the outer shell. Since it loses two negatively charged particles, the resulting ion has a positive charge of $2+$.

$$\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

highlights.text: Explain how magnesium forms an ion

steps.title: Describe the stable configuration
steps.content: Once those two electrons are gone, the magnesium ion ($Mg^{2+}$) is left with a full outer shell containing eight electrons (an **octet**). This electronic structure is identical to the noble gas **neon**, which is a highly stable arrangement. Achieving this 'noble gas configuration' is the reason magnesium forms an ion in the first place.

highlights.text: why it achieves a noble gas configuration

steps.title: Identify magnesium's electron configuration
steps.content:
To understand how magnesium forms an ion, we first look at its neutral ground state. Magnesium is the 12th element in the periodic table, meaning it has 12 electrons. Using subshell notation, we distribute these electrons into orbitals:

$$1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6 \, 3s^2$$

In this configuration, the outermost (valence) electrons are located in the $3s$ subshell.

steps.title: Determine how the ion forms
steps.content:
Magnesium is a metal in Group 2. Metals tend to lose their valence electrons to achieve a more stable state. To reach stability, magnesium loses the two electrons from its outermost $3s$ subshell. 

This process results in the formation of a magnesium ion with a $2+$ charge:

$$\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

The new subshell configuration for $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ is:

$$1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6$$

highlights.text: Magnesium loses two electrons from outer shell to form \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}

steps.title: Achieving a noble gas configuration
steps.content:
The resulting configuration, $1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^6$, contains a total of 10 electrons. This is identical to the electron configuration of **neon (Ne)**, which is a noble gas.

Noble gas configurations are exceptionally stable because they have a full outer shell (an octet in the $n=2$ level). By losing its $3s^2$ electrons, magnesium achieves this low-energy, stable state.

highlights.text: Achieves the stable electron configuration of neon (a noble gas)

parts.content: Deduce the formula of magnesium oxide based on the charges of its ions.
parts.markscheme:
Formula is $\mathrm{MgO}$ due to charges $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ balancing 1:1
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify Magnesium's charge
steps.content:
Magnesium ($\text{Mg}$) is located in **Group 2** of the periodic table. As a metal in this group, it tends to lose its two valence electrons to achieve a stable noble gas electron configuration. 

This loss of two electrons results in a magnesium ion with a $+2$ charge:
$$\text{Mg}^{2+}$$

This concept is part of **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify Oxygen's charge
steps.content:
Oxygen ($\text{O}$) is located in **Group 16** of the periodic table. As a non-metal, it needs to gain two electrons to complete its outer shell (octet). 

This gain of two electrons results in an oxide ion with a $-2$ charge:
$$\text{O}^{2-}$$

steps.title: Balance the charges
steps.content:
In an ionic compound, the total positive charge must exactly balance the total negative charge so that the compound is electrically neutral. 

Using the **Charge Balancing Approach**, we sum the charges:
$$\text{Total Charge} = (+2) + (-2) = 0$$

Because the charges are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, they cancel each other out in a $1:1$ ratio.

steps.title: State the final formula
steps.content:
Since one magnesium ion ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$) balances one oxide ion ($\text{O}^{2-}$), the simplest whole-number ratio of ions is $1:1$. 

Therefore, the chemical formula is:
$$\text{MgO}$$

highlights.text: Deduce the formula of magnesium oxide based on the charges of its ions.

steps.title: Identify the ions
steps.content:
To deduce the formula, we first need to identify the charges of the individual ions. Based on their positions in the Periodic Table:
- **Magnesium (Group 2)** loses two electrons to form a $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ cation.
- **Oxygen (Group 16)** gains two electrons to form an $\text{O}^{2-}$ anion.

This knowledge is part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: Deduce the formula of magnesium oxide based on the charges of its ions.

steps.title: Apply the Criss-Cross Method
steps.content:
Now, we apply the **Criss-Cross Method**. We take the numerical value of each ion's charge and swap them to become the subscript of the opposite element:

1. Write the symbols and charges: $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$.
2. Swap the numbers: the $2$ from the magnesium charge becomes the subscript for oxygen, and the $2$ from the oxygen charge becomes the subscript for magnesium.

This gives us an intermediate formula of $\text{Mg}_2\text{O}_2$.

steps.title: Simplify the ratio
steps.content:
Ionic compounds are always represented by their **empirical formula**, which is the simplest whole-number ratio of ions. 

Since we have a $2:2$ ratio, we simplify it by dividing both numbers by their greatest common divisor ($2$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mg} &= \frac{2}{2} = 1 \\ \text{O} &= \frac{2}{2} = 1 \end{aligned}$$

This results in a $1:1$ ratio, giving us the final formula: $\text{MgO}$.

gdc.instructions: Perform simple division to find the lowest whole-number ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide the subscripts by their common factor to simplify the ratio.

desmos.instructions: Divide the subscripts to find the simplified empirical ratio.

calculator.description: Simplify the ratio 2:2

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The formula of magnesium oxide is **$\text{MgO}$**. 

In the IB markscheme, you earn the mark by showing that the $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ charges balance in a $1:1$ ratio to create a neutral compound.

parts.content: Describe the structure of solid magnesium oxide.
parts.markscheme:
- Magnesium oxide forms a giant ionic lattice
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oppositely charged ions arranged in a regular, repeating 3D structure
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the lattice type
steps.content:
To describe the structure of magnesium oxide ($MgO$), we first identify that it is an ionic compound. In the solid state, ionic compounds do not form individual molecules. Instead, they form a **giant ionic lattice**.

This term "giant" indicates that the structure consists of a very large, indefinite number of ions bonded together throughout the entire crystal.

steps.title: Describe the spatial arrangement
steps.content:
The structure is characterized by a **regular, repeating three-dimensional (3D) arrangement** of ions. 

Specifically, it consists of **oppositely charged magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) and oxide ions ($O^{2-}$)** held together by strong electrostatic forces. These ions are packed together in a way that maximizes the attraction between opposite charges and minimizes the repulsion between like charges.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by including these key structural descriptions:

1. Stating that it forms a **giant ionic lattice**.
2. Describing the arrangement as a **regular, repeating 3D structure** of **oppositely charged ions**.

This knowledge is a fundamental part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

parts.content: Explain why magnesium oxide has a high melting point and is a good electrical insulator when solid.
parts.markscheme:
- High melting point due to strong electrostatic forces between ions requiring large energy to break
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solid does not conduct electricity because ions are fixed in lattice and cannot move freely
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the chemical structure
steps.content:
To understand the properties of Magnesium oxide ($MgO$), we first need to look at its structure. As an ionic compound, $MgO$ consists of a **giant ionic lattice** made up of alternating magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) and oxide ions ($O^{2-}$) held together in a regular 3D arrangement.

This topic is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Explain the melting point
steps.content:
The high melting point is a direct result of the **strong electrostatic forces of attraction** between the oppositely charged $Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$ ions. 

Because these attractions are very strong and occur throughout the entire lattice, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome them and allow the ions to move past one another (melting).

**Marking point:** You earn 1 mark for mentioning the strong electrostatic forces and the high energy needed to break them.

highlights.text: High melting point due to strong electrostatic forces between ions requiring large energy to break

steps.title: Explain the electrical insulation
steps.content:
For a substance to conduct electricity, it must contain mobile charged particles (like electrons or ions) that can move through the material. 

In **solid** magnesium oxide, the ions are held in **fixed positions** within the lattice structure. While they can vibrate, they cannot move from place to place. Since there are no mobile charge carriers, solid $MgO$ acts as an electrical insulator.

**Marking point:** You earn the 2nd mark for stating that ions are fixed in the lattice and cannot move freely.

highlights.text: Solid does not conduct electricity because ions are fixed in lattice and cannot move freely

options.content: Solid sodium chloride conducts electricity because its ions are free to move.
Molten magnesium oxide conducts electricity because its ions can move freely in the liquid state.
Solid calcium chloride conducts electricity because of the movement of electrons within its lattice.
Dissolved aluminum chloride in water does not conduct electricity because the ions are tightly bound.

parts.content: State the type of bonding present in sodium chloride and in carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$: Ionic bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $CCl_4$: Covalent bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze Sodium Chloride Elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first identify the types of elements involved using the periodic table (Topic S3.1):

- **Sodium ($Na$):** Located in Group 1, it is a metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** Located in Group 17, it is a non-metal.

This classification is a prerequisite found in **Structure 3: Classification of matter**.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $NaCl$
steps.content:
According to the **Elemental Classification** method, a bond formed between a metal and a non-metal is classified as an **ionic bond**. This occurs because the metal atom transfers electrons to the non-metal atom to achieve a stable electron configuration.

$$NaCl: \text{Ionic bonding}$$

steps.title: Analyze Carbon Tetrachloride Elements
steps.content:
Next, we look at the elements in carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):

- **Carbon ($C$):** A non-metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** A non-metal.

Identifying these elements correctly is key to using the **covalent model** covered in Topic S2.2.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
When two non-metals react, they share electron pairs rather than transferring them. The bond formed between two non-metals is classified as a **covalent bond**.

$$CCl_4: \text{Covalent bonding}$$

steps.title: Use Electronegativity Difference Approach
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type, we use the electronegativity values ($\chi$) from the IB Data Booklet. The difference in electronegativity ($\Delta\chi$) between two atoms tells us if the bond is ionic or covalent.

Generally:
- **$\Delta\chi > 1.8$**: Ionic bonding
- **$\Delta\chi < 1.8$**: Covalent bonding

This concept is part of **Topic S2.4: Bonding as a continuum**.

steps.title: Bonding in Sodium Chloride
steps.content:
Using the Pauling scale in the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{Na}) = 0.9$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for sodium chloride ($NaCl$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 0.9 = 2.3$$

Since $2.3$ is significantly greater than $1.8$, the bonding is **ionic**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract 0.9 from 3.2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for NaCl.

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
Using the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{C}) = 2.6$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 2.6 = 0.6$$

Since $0.6$ is much less than $1.8$, the bonding is **covalent**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract 2.6 from 3.2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 2.6 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 2.6 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for CCl4.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the electronegativity differences:
- **Sodium chloride:** Ionic bonding
- **Carbon tetrachloride:** Covalent bonding

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content:
The diagram shows a conductivity test involving a battery, a bulb, and electrodes. This setup allows us to deduce bonding types by observing whether a substance conducts electricity in its solid and molten (liquid) states. 

*   **Ionic compounds** conduct electricity when molten because their ions are free to move, but they do not conduct when solid because the ions are locked in a rigid lattice.
*   **Molecular covalent compounds** do not conduct in either state because they consist of neutral molecules with no free-moving charged particles (ions or electrons).

highlights.text: investigate the properties of sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride in both solid and molten states

steps.title: Deduce bonding for NaCl
steps.content:
Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is formed from a metal (Sodium) and a non-metal (Chlorine). In this experiment, $NaCl$ would cause the bulb to light up only when molten. This is because melting breaks the strong electrostatic attractions in the ionic lattice, allowing the $Na^{+}$ and $Cl^{-}$ ions to move toward the electrodes. This behavior is the classic signature of **ionic bonding**.

You can find more about this in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Deduce bonding for CCl4
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$) is composed of two non-metals (Carbon and Chlorine). These atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer shell. In the conductivity test, the bulb would not light up regardless of whether the substance is solid or liquid, because there are no ions or delocalized electrons to carry a charge. This identifies it as having **covalent bonding**.

Review **Topic S2.2: The covalent model** for more details on molecular structures.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on their physical properties and the elements involved:

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$):** Ionic bonding
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$):** Covalent bonding

parts.content: Explain why sodium chloride does not conduct electricity in the solid state but does when molten.
parts.markscheme:
- In solid $NaCl$, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In molten $NaCl$, ions are mobile and can carry charge. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the charge carriers
steps.content: To understand electrical conductivity in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first need to identify what carries the charge. $NaCl$ is an ionic compound made of sodium ions ($Na^+$) and chloride ions ($Cl^-$). For a substance to conduct electricity, it must contain particles that are both **charged** and **free to move**.

steps.title: Explain the solid state
steps.content:
In the solid state, $NaCl$ exists as a giant ionic lattice. The $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are held in fixed positions by strong electrostatic forces of attraction. 

Because the ions are **fixed in the lattice** and cannot move from place to place, they cannot carry an electric current. This is why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In solid NaCl, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move.

steps.title: Explain the molten state
steps.content:
When sodium chloride is heated until it becomes molten (liquid), the high temperature provides enough energy to overcome the strong electrostatic forces holding the lattice together. 

In this state, the lattice structure breaks down, and the **ions become mobile**. Since the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are now free to move throughout the liquid, they can carry the electric charge toward the electrodes, allowing the substance to conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In molten NaCl, ions are mobile and can carry charge.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both states:
1.  **Solid state:** Ions are in fixed positions in a lattice (1 mark).
2.  **Molten state:** Ions are free to move/mobile (1 mark).

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** in your IB Chemistry syllabus.

parts.content: Suggest why carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state.
parts.markscheme: $CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define electrical conductivity requirements
steps.content:
To understand why a substance conducts (or doesn't conduct) electricity, we use **Charge Carrier Analysis**. For any material to conduct an electric current, it must possess particles that are both **charged** and **mobile** (free to move). These charge carriers are typically either:

1.  **Delocalized electrons** (common in metals and graphite).
2.  **Free-moving ions** (common in molten or aqueous ionic compounds).

steps.title: Analyze the bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple molecular covalent** compound. In this structure, carbon and chlorine atoms are held together by localized covalent bonds where electrons are shared between specific atoms. 

This means:
- There are no ions present because the atoms are not losing or gaining electrons to form charged particles.
- There are no delocalized electrons because all valence electrons are either involved in specific bonds or localized as lone pairs on the chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Apply Charge Carrier Analysis
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ consists of neutral molecules, there are no mobile ions to carry charge. Even in the molten (liquid) state, the molecules remain neutral and the electrons remain localized within the covalent bonds. 

Because it lacks both **free-moving ions** and **delocalized electrons**, it cannot conduct electricity in any state.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state

steps.title: Final Markscheme Alignment
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you simply need to state the absence of these carriers.

**Answer:**
$CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: To understand why a substance conducts or doesn't conduct electricity, we first look at its bonding. Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple covalent molecular substance**. It is formed from two non-metals (carbon and chlorine) sharing electrons to form covalent bonds.

steps.title: Analyze the charge carriers
steps.content:
For electricity to flow, a substance must have **mobile charged particles**, such as free-moving ions or delocalized electrons. 

In $CCl_4$:
1. The substance consists of **neutral molecules**. There are no ions present in the solid or liquid (molten) state.
2. The valence electrons are **localized** within the specific covalent bonds between the carbon and chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Relate structure to conductivity
steps.content:
Because the molecules are neutral and the electrons are held tightly within the bonds, there are no charged particles available to move and carry a current. 

This is why $CCl_4$ remains a non-conductor in any state, unlike ionic compounds (like $NaCl$) which can conduct when molten because their ions become free to move.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to state that the substance lacks the necessary mobile charges.

**Final Answer:** Carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity because it has **no free-moving (mobile) ions or electrons**.

parts.content: Use the diagram and your knowledge of structure and bonding to deduce which substance(s) contain mobile ions.
parts.markscheme: Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding types
steps.content:
To determine which substance has mobile ions, we first need to look at their bonding. 

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$)** is an **ionic compound** formed between a metal (Na) and a non-metal (Cl). It consists of a giant lattice of positive sodium ions ($Na^+$) and negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$).
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$)** is a **covalent molecular compound** formed between two non-metals. It consists of neutral molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces.

This fundamental difference is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** and **Structure 2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Evaluate ion mobility
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the state of these substances. 

In **solid $NaCl$**, the ions are locked into a rigid 3D lattice by strong electrostatic forces. Because they are in fixed positions, they cannot move to carry a charge. However, when $NaCl$ is **molten** (melted), the lattice structure breaks down, and the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are free to move throughout the liquid.

In contrast, $CCl_4$ is made of neutral molecules. Whether it is solid or molten, it does not contain ions at all, only molecules.

steps.title: Deduce the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:
1. $CCl_4$ has no ions in either state.
2. Solid $NaCl$ has ions, but they are not mobile.
3. **Molten $NaCl$** has ions that are free to move.

Therefore, the only substance containing mobile ions is molten $NaCl$.

steps.title: Analyze $CCl_4$ properties
steps.content: First, let's look at carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). It is a covalent molecular substance composed of neutral molecules. Because it does not contain any ions in either its solid or molten state, we can eliminate it immediately from the list of substances containing mobile ions.

steps.title: Evaluate solid $NaCl$
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is an ionic compound containing $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. However, in its solid state, these ions are locked in a rigid 3D lattice structure. While the ions exist, they are not **mobile** because they are held firmly in place by strong electrostatic attractions.

steps.title: Identify the mobile phase
steps.content: When $NaCl$ is heated to its melting point to become molten, the giant ionic lattice breaks down. This allows the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions to move freely throughout the liquid. Therefore, only molten $NaCl$ contains ions that are actually mobile.

steps.title: Final Deduction
steps.content:
By ruling out the molecular substance ($CCl_4$) and the fixed lattice structure (solid $NaCl$), we conclude that only molten $NaCl$ fits the criteria.

**Final Answer:** Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions.

parts.content: State and explain the difference in melting point between sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$ has a much higher melting point than $CCl_4$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Due to strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice vs. weak London forces between $CCl_4$ molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: State the difference
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) has a significantly higher melting point than carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). To understand why, we need to look at how their atoms are held together in the solid state.

highlights.text: NaCl has a much higher melting point than CCl4

steps.title: Analyze sodium chloride structure
steps.content:
Sodium chloride is an ionic compound that exists as a **giant ionic lattice**. In this structure, ions are held together by **strong electrostatic attractions** between oppositely charged $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. 

Because these attractions are very strong and extend throughout the entire 3D lattice, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome them and melt the solid.

highlights.text: strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice

steps.title: Analyze carbon tetrachloride structure
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride is a **simple molecular** substance. While the $C-Cl$ bonds within the molecule are strong covalent bonds, the forces holding the $CCl_4$ molecules to each other in a solid are **weak London dispersion forces** (intermolecular forces).

Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, these London forces are the only attractions present between molecules.

highlights.text: weak London forces between CCl4 molecules

steps.title: Contrast the energy required
steps.content:
The difference in melting point comes down to the energy needed to disrupt these different types of forces:

1.  In **$NaCl$**, you must break strong ionic bonds within a giant lattice.
2.  In **$CCl_4$**, you only need to overcome weak intermolecular forces between molecules.

Therefore, $NaCl$ requires much more energy to melt, resulting in a much higher melting point. This comparison is a core concept in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

parts.content: Explain why carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature. Justify your answer based on its intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze molecular polarity
steps.content:
To determine the intermolecular forces, we first look at the structure of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). Although the individual $C-Cl$ bonds are polar due to the difference in electronegativity, the molecule has a **symmetrical tetrahedral shape**. 

Because of this symmetry, the bond dipoles cancel each other out, making the $CCl_4$ molecule **non-polar**. This is a key prerequisite concept from **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, the only type of intermolecular forces it can form are **London dispersion forces** (also known as instantaneous induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

These forces occur because of the constant movement of electrons, which creates temporary dipoles that induce dipoles in neighboring molecules.

steps.title: Explain the liquid state
steps.content:
At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the molecules have a certain amount of kinetic energy. For $CCl_4$, this energy is **insufficient to completely overcome** the London dispersion forces between the molecules. 

Because the forces are strong enough to keep the molecules close together but weak enough to allow them to slide past each other, the substance exists as a **liquid** rather than a gas. 

**Markscheme Note:** 
- Mentioning **London dispersion forces** earns the first mark.
- Stating that the molecules are **non-polar** or that there is **insufficient energy to overcome these forces** at room temperature earns the second mark.

steps.title: Identify the intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a non-polar molecule because of its symmetrical tetrahedral shape. Because it is non-polar, the only intermolecular forces acting between its molecules are weak **London dispersion forces** (also known as van der Waals forces or induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

This is the first key point needed to earn marks according to the markscheme.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature

steps.title: Apply energy-state relationship
steps.content:
The state of matter is determined by the balance between the thermal (kinetic) energy of the particles and the strength of the intermolecular forces holding them together. 

At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the average kinetic energy provided to the molecules is:
1.  **Enough** to overcome the rigid lattice structure of a solid, allowing the molecules to move and flow.
2.  **Insufficient** to completely overcome the London dispersion forces, meaning the molecules remain close together rather than flying apart as a gas.

Because the energy is enough to overcome some but not all of the forces, $CCl_4$ exists as a **liquid**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your answer should connect these two points:

*   **Mark 1:** State that $CCl_4$ molecules are held together by weak London dispersion forces.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain that at room temperature, there is insufficient energy to fully overcome these forces to form a gas, resulting in the liquid state.

This concept is covered in **Structure 2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content: To understand the properties of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$), we first look at the individual bonds. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, which creates a polar covalent bond where the electron density is pulled towards the chlorine atoms. This creates individual bond dipoles directed from $C$ to $Cl$.

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content: According to VSEPR theory (Topic S2.2.4), carbon has four bonding electron domains and no lone pairs, resulting in a **tetrahedral** geometry. Because all four atoms surrounding the central carbon are identical (chlorine), the molecule is perfectly symmetrical.

steps.title: Determine molecular polarity
steps.content: Due to the tetrahedral symmetry, the individual polar bond dipoles cancel each other out exactly. As a result, $CCl_4$ is a **non-polar molecule**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Because the molecules are non-polar, the only attractions between them are **weak London dispersion forces**. These forces arise from temporary induced dipoles. 

In the IB markscheme, identifying these forces earns the first mark.

highlights.text: Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules.

steps.title: Relate forces to state
steps.content:
At room temperature, there is insufficient thermal energy to completely overcome these intermolecular forces to turn the substance into a gas, but the forces are weak enough to allow the molecules to slide past one another. Therefore, $CCl_4$ exists as a liquid.

Stating that these forces occur between non-polar molecules earns the second mark.

highlights.text: The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis (electron dot) structure for the $Na^+$ ion and a $CCl_4$ molecule
parts.markscheme:
$Na^+$: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost) <marks>1 mark</marks>

$CCl_4$: carbon single bonded to four $Cl$ atoms; each $Cl$ with 3 lone pairs <marks>1 mark</marks>


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7e8a691d-b87a-4c65-9943-1ee90d7d96eb)

steps.title: Analyze $Na^+$ ion structure
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) is a Group 1 element with 1 valence electron. To form the $Na^+$ ion, it loses this single electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (matching Neon). 

In IB chemistry, when a metal ion loses all its valence electrons, the Lewis structure is drawn by simply showing the element's symbol and its charge. No dots are placed around it because the original valence shell is now empty.

$$Na^+$$

steps.title: Count $CCl_4$ valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw the $CCl_4$ molecule, we first calculate the total number of valence electrons available:

- Carbon ($C$) is in Group 14: **4 electrons**
- Chlorine ($Cl$) is in Group 17: **7 electrons per atom**

$$\text{Total} = 4 + (4 \times 7) = 32 \text{ electrons}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the sum of valence electrons.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the total.

calculator.description: Calculate total valence electrons for carbon tetrachloride.

steps.title: Distribute electrons in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Now we systematically distribute the 32 electrons:

1.  **Form the skeleton**: Place $C$ in the center and connect the four $Cl$ atoms with single bonds. Each bond uses 2 electrons, so $4 \times 2 = 8$ electrons used.
2.  **Complete octets**: Subtract the bonding electrons from the total ($32 - 8 = 24$). Place these 24 electrons as lone pairs around the $Cl$ atoms. Each $Cl$ needs 6 more electrons (3 lone pairs) to complete its octet.
3.  **Check Carbon**: The central Carbon already has 4 bonds (8 electrons), so its octet is satisfied.

Every atom now has a full valence shell using exactly 32 electrons.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the 8 bonding electrons.

ti84.instructions: Subtract bonding electrons from total.

desmos.instructions: Calculate remaining electrons.

calculator.description: Verify remaining electrons for lone pairs.

steps.title: Final Lewis Structures
steps.content:
Combining the steps, your final diagrams should look like this:

- **$Na^+$**: Just the symbol and the charge: $$Na^+$$
- **$CCl_4$**: A central $C$ with four single bonds to $Cl$, and each $Cl$ surrounded by 6 dots (3 lone pairs).

$$\begin{matrix} & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \\ & & | & & \\ :\ddot{Cl} & - & C & - & \ddot{Cl}: \\ & & | & & \\ & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: Na+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)
CCl_4: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms; each Cl with 3 lone pairs

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw Lewis structures, we first determine the number of valence electrons for each atom using their group numbers in the periodic table:

*   **Sodium (Na)**: Group 1, so it has 1 valence electron.
*   **Carbon (C)**: Group 14, so it has 4 valence electrons.
*   **Chlorine (Cl)**: Group 17, so it has 7 valence electrons.

This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Draw the Sodium Ion
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) forms an ion by losing its single valence electron to achieve a stable, full inner shell. 

In a Lewis structure for a positive ion (cation), we show the symbol and the charge. Since the original third-shell electron is gone, the diagram shows no dots or crosses around the symbol.

$$[Na]^+$$

highlights.text: Na^+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
For $CCl_4$, Carbon is the central atom. It uses its 4 valence electrons (represented as crosses $\times$) to form four single covalent bonds with four Chlorine atoms.

Each Chlorine atom provides 1 electron (represented as a dot $\bullet$) to share with Carbon, creating four bonding pairs. This allows Carbon to reach a stable octet (8 electrons).

$$\begin{matrix} & \text{Cl} & \\ \text{Cl} & \text{C} & \text{Cl} \\ & \text{Cl} & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms

steps.title: Complete Chlorine's lone pairs
steps.content:
Each Chlorine atom starts with 7 valence electrons. Since it shares only 1 electron with Carbon, it has 6 non-bonding electrons remaining. These must be drawn as **3 lone pairs** (pairs of dots $\bullet \bullet$) around each Chlorine atom.

This ensures every Chlorine atom also satisfies the octet rule ($2$ shared $+ 6$ non-bonding $= 8$ electrons).

**Final Dot and Cross Representation:**
$$\begin{array}{ccc} & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \times \bullet & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet & \bullet \text{C} \times & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \bullet \times & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \end{array}$$

- $\text{NaCl}$: $\text{Na}^+$ (charge $+1$, larger radius), $\text{Cl}^-$ (charge $-1$, larger radius)
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$: $\text{Al}^{3+}$ (charge $+3$, smaller radius), $\text{O}^{2-}$ (charge $-2$, smaller radius compared to $\text{Cl}^-$)
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Determine ionic charges
steps.content:
To start, we look at the positions of the elements in the periodic table to determine their ionic charges. This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

- **Sodium ($Na$):** Group 1 element, loses 1 electron to form $Na^+$.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** Group 17 element, gains 1 electron to form $Cl^-$.
- **Aluminum ($Al$):** Group 13 element, loses 3 electrons to form $Al^{3+}$.
- **Oxygen ($O$):** Group 16 element, gains 2 electrons to form $O^{2-}$.

This gives us our first point of comparison: the ions in $Al_2O_3$ have significantly higher charges ($+3$ and $-2$) than those in $NaCl$ ($+1$ and $-1$).

steps.title: Compare sizes by shells
steps.content:
Next, we compare the sizes (ionic radii) by looking at the number of occupied electron shells. This follows the trends in **Topic S3.1: Periodicity**.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{Ion} && \text{Electron Configuration} && \text{Occupied Shells} \\ &Cl^- && 2, 8, 8 && 3 \\ &Na^+ && 2, 8 && 2 \\ &O^{2-} && 2, 8 && 2 \\ &Al^{3+} && 2, 8 && 2 \end{aligned}$$

Because $Cl^-$ has 3 occupied shells, it is significantly larger than the other ions. $O^{2-}$, having only 2 shells, is smaller than $Cl^-$.

steps.title: Compare isoelectronic ion sizes
steps.content:
For ions with the same number of shells (isoelectronic ions like $Na^+$, $Al^{3+}$, and $O^{2-}$), we compare their **effective nuclear charge** ($Z_{eff}$).

- **$Na^+$ (Z=11):** 11 protons pulling on 10 electrons.
- **$Al^{3+}$ (Z=13):** 13 protons pulling on 10 electrons.

Since aluminum has more protons in its nucleus, it exerts a stronger pull on the same number of electrons, making the $Al^{3+}$ ion smaller than the $Na^+$ ion. Similarly, $O^{2-}$ (Z=8) has fewer protons and is larger than $Al^{3+}$.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, you must clearly state the charge and relative size for both compounds:

- **For $NaCl$ (1 mark):** The ions are $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$. These have lower charges ($+1, -1$) and larger radii (especially $Cl^-$ with 3 shells).
- **For $Al_2O_3$ (1 mark):** The ions are $Al^{3+}$ and $O^{2-}$. These have higher charges ($+3, -2$) and smaller radii compared to their counterparts in $NaCl$.

steps.title: Identify the ions and charges
steps.content:
First, we need to identify the ions formed in each compound and their respective charges. Sodium chloride and aluminum oxide are both ionic compounds formed from metals and non-metals.

- In $\text{NaCl}$, the ions are $\text{Na}^{+}$ (sodium ion) and $\text{Cl}^{-}$ (chloride ion).
- In $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, the ions are $\text{Al}^{3+}$ (aluminum ion) and $\text{O}^{2-}$ (oxide ion).

This gives us our first set of data: the charges for $\text{NaCl}$ are $+1$ and $-1$, while for $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, they are $+3$ and $-2$.

steps.title: Determine electron counts
steps.content:
To compare their sizes, we look at their electron configurations. An **isoelectronic series** consists of ions that have the same number of electrons.

Using the atomic numbers ($Z$) from the periodic table:
- $\text{Na}^{+}$ ($Z=11$): $11 - 1 = 10$ electrons
- $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ($Z=13$): $13 - 3 = 10$ electrons
- $\text{O}^{2-}$ ($Z=8$): $8 + 2 = 10$ electrons
- $\text{Cl}^{-}$ ($Z=17$): $17 + 1 = 18$ electrons

We can see that $\text{Na}^{+}$, $\text{Al}^{3+}$, and $\text{O}^{2-}$ are all isoelectronic, each having 10 electrons (the configuration of Neon).

gdc.instructions: Subtract the charge from the atomic number for cations; add the absolute value of the charge for anions.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the charge from the atomic number for cations; add the absolute value of the charge for anions.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the charge from the atomic number for cations; add the absolute value of the charge for anions.

calculator.description: Verify electron counts for the ions

steps.title: Apply isoelectronic series analysis
steps.content:
For isoelectronic ions, the ionic radius is determined by the **nuclear charge** ($Z$). The more protons in the nucleus, the stronger the attraction for the 10 electrons, pulling them closer and making the ion smaller.

Comparing the three isoelectronic ions:
- $\text{Al}^{3+}$ has 13 protons: strongest pull, **smallest** radius.
- $\text{Na}^{+}$ has 11 protons: moderate pull, medium radius.
- $\text{O}^{2-}$ has 8 protons: weakest pull, **largest** radius among these three.

Therefore, the ions in $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (especially $\text{Al}^{3+}$) are significantly smaller than the sodium ion.

steps.title: Account for additional shells
steps.content:
Now we consider $\text{Cl}^{-}$. Unlike the other three ions, $\text{Cl}^{-}$ has 18 electrons. This means its electrons occupy **three** main energy levels ($n=3$), whereas the isoelectronic group only occupies **two** ($n=2$).

Because it has an additional shell of electrons, $\text{Cl}^{-}$ is much larger than $\text{O}^{2-}$, $\text{Na}^{+}$, and $\text{Al}^{3+}$.

steps.title: Summarize the final comparison
steps.content:
Based on our analysis, we can conclude:

1.  **For $\text{NaCl}$:** The ions ($\text{Na}^{+}$ and $\text{Cl}^{-}$) have lower charges ($+1, -1$) and larger radii (especially the chloride ion with its extra shell).
2.  **For $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$:** The ions ($\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$) have higher charges ($+3, -2$) and smaller radii because they have fewer shells and $\text{Al}^{3+}$ has a high nuclear charge.

This explains why $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ has a much higher lattice enthalpy and melting point than $\text{NaCl}$.

steps.title: Identify ions and charges
steps.content:
First, we need to identify the constituent ions for each compound based on their positions in the Periodic Table.

*   **Sodium chloride (NaCl):** Sodium is in Group 1, so it forms the $\text{Na}^{+}$ ion ($+1$ charge). Chlorine is in Group 17, so it forms the $\text{Cl}^{-}$ ion ($-1$ charge).
*   **Aluminum oxide ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$):** Aluminum is in Group 13, so it forms the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ion ($+3$ charge). Oxygen is in Group 16, so it forms the $\text{O}^{2-}$ ion ($-2$ charge).

This shows that the ions in $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ have higher formal charges than those in $\text{NaCl}$.

steps.title: Reference ionic radii data
steps.content: Now, let's look at **Section 9** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet to find the specific ionic radii for these ions to compare their sizes.

steps.title: Compare Sodium Chloride ions
steps.content:
From Section 9 of the Data Booklet, we find the following values for $\text{NaCl}$:

*   **Sodium ion ($\text{Na}^{+}$):** $102 \text{ pm}$
*   **Chloride ion ($\text{Cl}^{-}$):** $181 \text{ pm}$

These ions are relatively larger than their counterparts in Aluminum oxide.

steps.title: Compare Aluminum Oxide ions
steps.content:
Next, we look up the values for $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in the same section:

*   **Aluminum ion ($\text{Al}^{3+}$):** $53.5 \text{ pm}$
*   **Oxide ion ($\text{O}^{2-}$):** $140 \text{ pm}$

Comparing these results:
1.  $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ($53.5 \text{ pm}$) is significantly smaller than $\text{Na}^{+}$ ($102 \text{ pm}$) because it is isoelectronic but has more protons ($13$ vs $11$), pulling the electrons closer.
2.  $\text{O}^{2-}$ ($140 \text{ pm}$) is smaller than $\text{Cl}^{-}$ ($181 \text{ pm}$) because it only has electrons in the second energy level ($n=2$), whereas $\text{Cl}^{-}$ has an occupied third energy level ($n=3$).

steps.title: Summarize the comparison
steps.content:
To earn both marks, clearly state the comparison of both charge and size:

*   **$\text{NaCl}$**: Has lower charges ($+1, -1$) and larger ionic radii ($\text{Na}^+ = 102 \text{ pm}$, $\text{Cl}^- = 181 \text{ pm}$).
*   **$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$**: Has higher charges ($+3, -2$) and smaller ionic radii ($\text{Al}^{3+} = 53.5 \text{ pm}$, $\text{O}^{2-} = 140 \text{ pm}$).

parts.content: Predict which compound has the higher melting point and justify your answer using ionic bonding theory.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ has higher melting point
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Due to stronger ionic bonds requiring more energy to break
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Make the prediction
steps.content: First, we need to identify which compound has the higher melting point. Based on their chemical compositions, **aluminum oxide ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$)** has a much higher melting point than sodium chloride ($\text{NaCl}$).

steps.title: Compare ionic charges
steps.content:
To justify this, let's look at the charges of the ions involved. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**:

*   In **$\text{NaCl}$**, the ions are $\text{Na}^{+}$ and $\text{Cl}^{-}$.
*   In **$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$**, the ions are $\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$.

The ions in aluminum oxide have significantly higher charges ($3+$ and $2-$) compared to the single charges ($1+$ and $1-$) in sodium chloride.

steps.title: Analyze electrostatic attraction
steps.content:
Ionic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions. The strength of this attraction is proportional to the product of the ionic charges ($q_1 \times q_2$).

Because the charges in $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ are greater, the **electrostatic attraction** between the ions is much stronger than in $\text{NaCl}$.

steps.title: Link to melting point
steps.content:
Melting occurs when enough energy is supplied to overcome the attractions within the ionic lattice. 

Since the ionic bonds in $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ are stronger due to the higher charges, **more energy is required to break the lattice**, resulting in a higher melting point. 

In an exam, you earn the second mark by explicitly stating that these stronger bonds require more energy to break.

highlights.text: Due to stronger ionic bonds requiring more energy to break

steps.title: Predict the compound
steps.content: Based on ionic bonding theory, aluminum oxide ($Al_2O_3$) has a significantly higher melting point than sodium chloride ($NaCl$).

steps.title: Compare ionic charges
steps.content:
The first factor affecting lattice enthalpy is the charge of the ions. In $NaCl$, the ions are $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ (charges of $+1$ and $-1$). In $Al_2O_3$, the ions are $Al^{3+}$ and $O^{2-}$ (charges of $+3$ and $-2$). 

The product of the charges in $Al_2O_3$ ($3 \times 2 = 6$) is much greater than in $NaCl$ ($1 \times 1 = 1$), leading to much stronger electrostatic attractions.

steps.title: Compare ionic radii
steps.content:
The second factor is the size of the ions. $Al^{3+}$ is smaller than $Na^+$, and $O^{2-}$ is smaller than $Cl^-$. 

Smaller ionic radii allow the ions to get closer together. Since the strength of the attraction is inversely proportional to the distance between the centers of the ions, smaller radii result in stronger bonds.

steps.title: Relate to lattice enthalpy
steps.content:
Because $Al_2O_3$ has both **higher ionic charges** and **smaller ionic radii**, it has a much higher **lattice enthalpy** than $NaCl$. 

A higher lattice enthalpy indicates a more stable lattice with stronger ionic bonds, which requires significantly more energy to overcome during the melting process.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Therefore, the melting point of $Al_2O_3$ (approx. $2072^\circ\text{C}$) is much higher than that of $NaCl$ (approx. $801^\circ\text{C}$).

**Marks breakdown:**
- 1 mark for identifying $Al_2O_3$.
- 1 mark for justifying with stronger ionic bonds/higher lattice enthalpy due to charge and radius factors.

parts.content: Explain why solid $\text{NaCl}$ does not conduct electricity but molten $\text{NaCl}$ does.
parts.markscheme:
- Solid $\text{NaCl}$ ions are fixed in lattice $\rightarrow$ cannot move $\rightarrow$ no conductivity
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molten $\text{NaCl}$ ions are free to move $\rightarrow$ conducts electricity
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define conductivity requirements
steps.content:
To understand why a substance conducts electricity, we first need to identify the requirements for electrical flow. For any material to conduct electricity, it must contain **charged particles** (like ions or electrons) that are **free to move** through the structure. 

In ionic compounds like $\text{NaCl}$, the charged particles are the sodium ions ($Na^+$) and chloride ions ($Cl^-$). This concept is explored in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Analyze the solid state
steps.content:
In solid $\text{NaCl}$, the ions are arranged in a regular, three-dimensional arrangement called a **giant ionic lattice**. 

Because the electrostatic attractions between the oppositely charged ions are very strong, the ions are held in **fixed positions** within the lattice. Since these ions cannot move from their fixed spots, they cannot carry an electric charge from one place to another, and the solid does not conduct electricity.

highlights.text: Solid NaCl ions are fixed in lattice

steps.title: Analyze the molten state
steps.content:
When $\text{NaCl}$ is heated until it becomes **molten** (liquid), the high temperature provides enough energy to overcome the strong electrostatic forces holding the lattice together. 

In this state, the lattice structure breaks down, and the ions are now **free to move** (mobile). When an electric potential is applied, these mobile ions can migrate toward the electrodes, allowing the substance to conduct electricity.

highlights.text: Molten NaCl ions are free to move

steps.title: Summary of findings
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam:
1. In the **solid**, ions are in fixed positions in the lattice and cannot move, so there is no conductivity. (1 mark)
2. In the **molten** state, the ions are free to move, which allows for the conduction of electricity. (1 mark)

parts.content: Write the electron configuration of a neutral sodium atom and a sodium ion.
parts.markscheme:
- sodium atom: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- sodium ion: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify number of electrons
steps.content:
To write the electron configuration, we first need to know how many electrons we are dealing with. Using the Periodic Table, we find that Sodium ($Na$) has an atomic number of 11. For a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the atomic number.

This falls under **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**, where we learn that electrons fill subshells based on increasing energy levels.

highlights.text: Write the electron configuration of a neutral sodium atom

steps.title: Neutral atom configuration
steps.content:
Using the Aufbau principle, we fill the subshells in order of increasing energy: $1s$, then $2s$, then $2p$, and finally $3s$. 

- The $s$ subshells hold a maximum of 2 electrons.
- The $p$ subshells hold a maximum of 6 electrons.

For 11 electrons, the full spectroscopic notation is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

This earns you the first mark.

gdc.instructions: Sum the electrons in each subshell: 2 + 2 + 6 + 1

ti84.instructions: Add the exponents: 2 + 2 + 6 + 1

desmos.instructions: Calculate the sum of electrons: 2 + 2 + 6 + 1

calculator.description: Verify total electron count for the neutral atom

steps.title: Determine ion electron count
steps.content:
Next, we consider the sodium ion ($Na^+$). The positive charge indicates that the neutral atom has lost one electron. 

Since sodium is in Group 1, it loses its single valence electron from the outermost shell ($3s$) to achieve a stable octet configuration.

ti84.instructions: Subtract 1 from the original 11 electrons.

calculator.description: Calculate electrons remaining in the ion

steps.title: Sodium ion configuration
steps.content:
By removing the $3s^1$ electron from the neutral atom's configuration, we are left with 10 electrons. The full spectroscopic notation for the sodium ion is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

This corresponds to the second mark.

ti84.instructions: Add the exponents: 2 + 2 + 6

calculator.description: Verify total electron count for the ion

steps.title: Locate Sodium and Preceding Noble Gas
steps.content:
To write the electron configuration using the **Noble Gas Shorthand** approach, we first look at the periodic table to find Sodium ($Na$). Sodium has an atomic number of 11, meaning a neutral atom has 11 electrons.

The noble gas that immediately precedes Sodium is **Neon ($Ne$)**, which has an atomic number of 10. This noble gas represents the stable "core" electrons of the sodium atom.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Define the Noble Gas Core
steps.content:
The symbol $[Ne]$ represents the electron configuration of Neon, which accounts for the first 10 electrons in the $n=1$ and $n=2$ energy levels:

$$[Ne] = 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

By using this shorthand, we can focus on the valence (outer) electrons that determine the atom's chemical properties.

steps.title: Configuration for Sodium Atom
steps.content:
Since Sodium has 11 electrons and the Neon core $[Ne]$ accounts for 10, there is 1 remaining electron to place. 

This 11th electron enters the next available energy level, which is the $3s$ sublevel. Therefore, the configuration for a **neutral sodium atom** is:

$$[Ne] 3s^1$$

To match the full notation required by the markscheme:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

gdc.instructions: Add the core electrons (10) and the valence electron (1).

ti84.instructions: Add the electrons in the core [Ne] (10) to the valence electron in 3s (1).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the total number of electrons.

calculator.description: Verify electron count for neutral Na

steps.title: Configuration for Sodium Ion
steps.content:
To form a sodium ion ($Na^+$), the atom loses its single valence electron from the $3s$ orbital to achieve a stable octet (the same configuration as a noble gas).

Starting from $[Ne] 3s^1$ and removing the $3s^1$ electron, we are left with just the core:

$$[Ne]$$

In full notation, the **sodium ion** configuration is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract 1 electron from 11.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the lost electron (1) from the neutral atom total (11).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the total number of electrons in the ion.

calculator.description: Verify electron count for Na+ ion

steps.title: Identify the atomic number
steps.content:
First, we look at the Periodic Table to find the atomic number of sodium ($Na$). Sodium is the 11th element, which means a neutral sodium atom has 11 protons and **11 electrons**.

This falls under **Structure 1.3: Electron configurations** in your syllabus. Understanding how electrons fill sublevels is essential for this question.

steps.title: Configure the neutral atom
steps.content:
Following the Aufbau principle (filling lower energy levels first), we distribute the 11 electrons into orbitals:

- The $1s$ orbital takes 2 electrons.
- The $2s$ orbital takes 2 electrons.
- The $2p$ orbital takes 6 electrons.
- The remaining 1 electron goes into the $3s$ orbital.

So, the configuration for a **sodium atom** is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

In the IB markscheme, writing this correctly earns you your first mark **(A1)**.

steps.title: Determine ion electron count
steps.content:
Sodium is in Group 1, so it loses its one valence electron (the $3s^1$ electron) to form a stable **sodium ion** ($Na^+$) with a full outer shell. 

Since it started with 11 electrons and lost 1, the $Na^+$ ion has **10 electrons** total.

steps.title: Apply isoelectronic determination
steps.content:
An easy way to find the ion's configuration is to identify which noble gas is **isoelectronic** with it (meaning they have the same number of electrons). 

The noble gas with 10 electrons is **Neon ($Ne$)**. Since $Na^+$ and $Ne$ are isoelectronic, they share the same electron configuration.

steps.title: State the ion configuration
steps.content:
The electron configuration for Neon (and therefore the **sodium ion**) is the neutral sodium configuration without the $3s^1$ electron:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

Providing this final configuration earns you the second mark **(A1)**.

parts.content: Explain how sodium forms a positive ion and why it is stable.
parts.markscheme:
- sodium loses one electron from its valence/3s subshell <marks>A1</marks>
- achieves a full octet/noble gas configuration / electron configuration of neon <marks>R1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify sodium's electron configuration
steps.content:
To understand how sodium forms an ion, we first look at the electron configuration of a neutral sodium atom ($Na$). Sodium has an atomic number of 11, meaning it has 11 electrons.

Its ground-state electron configuration is:
$$1s^{2} 2s^{2} 2p^{6} 3s^{1}$$

This shows that sodium has one valence electron located in the $3s$ subshell.

steps.title: Explain the ionization process
steps.content:
Sodium forms a positive ion ($Na^{+}$) by losing its single valence electron. Specifically, this electron is removed from the $3s$ subshell.

$$\text{Na} \rightarrow \text{Na}^{+} + e^{-}$$

highlights.text: sodium loses one electron from its valence/3s subshell

steps.title: Analyze the resulting stability
steps.content:
Once the $3s^{1}$ electron is lost, the electron configuration of the sodium ion ($Na^{+}$) becomes:
$$1s^{2} 2s^{2} 2p^{6}$$

This configuration is identical to that of the noble gas **neon**. Having a full outer shell (a complete octet in the second main energy level) is a low-energy, highly stable state, which explains why the $Na^{+}$ ion is so stable.

highlights.text: achieves a full octet/noble gas configuration / electron configuration of neon

steps.title: Identify sodium's valence electrons
steps.content:
To understand how sodium ($Na$) forms an ion, we first look at its position in the Periodic Table. Sodium is in **Group 1**, meaning it has **one valence electron** in its outermost shell. 

Its ground-state electron configuration is:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

You can review this in **Structure 1.3: Electron configurations** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Loss of valence electron
steps.content:
Sodium forms a positive ion by **losing its single valence electron** from the $3s$ subshell. Because it loses one negatively charged electron while the number of protons in the nucleus remains the same (11), the resulting particle has a $1+$ charge.

$$\text{Na} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + e^-$$

highlights.text: sodium loses one electron from its valence/3s subshell

steps.title: Stability and the octet
steps.content:
The resulting $Na^+$ ion is stable because it now has the electron configuration:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

This is a **full octet** (8 electrons in the $n=2$ shell), which is the same configuration as the noble gas **Neon**. According to the octet rule, atoms are most stable when they have a full outer shell, as this represents a state of lower potential energy.

highlights.text: achieves a full octet/noble gas configuration / electron configuration of neon

parts.content: Write the formula of sodium oxide based on the charges of the ions formed.
parts.markscheme:
- $Na_2O$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the ion charges
steps.content:
To write the formula for sodium oxide, we first determine the charges of the individual ions based on their position in the periodic table (Topic **S3.1: The periodic table**):

- **Sodium (Na)** is in Group 1, so it loses one electron to form a cation with a charge of $1+$, written as $Na^+$.
- **Oxygen (O)** is in Group 16, so it gains two electrons to form an anion with a charge of $2-$, written as $O^{2-}$.

highlights.text: Write the formula of sodium oxide

steps.title: Apply charge balancing principle
steps.content:
In an ionic compound, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge so that the overall compound is electrically neutral (charge = 0).

Let $x$ be the number of $Na^+$ ions and $y$ be the number of $O^{2-}$ ions. The balancing equation is:

$$x(+1) + y(-2) = 0$$

steps.title: Determine the simplest ratio
steps.content:
To find the smallest whole-number ratio that satisfies the equation $x - 2y = 0$, we can see that if we have 2 sodium ions for every 1 oxygen ion, the charges cancel out perfectly:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total positive charge} &= 2 \times (+1) = +2 \\ \text{Total negative charge} &= 1 \times (-2) = -2 \\ \text{Net charge} &= (+2) + (-2) = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write the final formula
steps.content:
Since the ratio of $Na$ to $O$ is $2:1$, we write the number of atoms as subscripts in the chemical formula. 

The formula for sodium oxide is **$Na_2O$**.

steps.title: Identify ion charges
steps.content:
To write the formula for **sodium oxide**, we first identify the charges of the individual ions based on their positions in the Periodic Table (Topic S3.1):

- **Sodium ($Na$)** is in Group 1, so it loses one electron to form a $1+$ ion: $Na^{1+}$.
- **Oxygen ($O$)** is in Group 16, so it gains two electrons to form a $2-$ ion: $O^{2-}$.

steps.title: Set up the crossover
steps.content:
Write the symbols of the ions side-by-side with their respective charges written as superscripts:

$$Na^{1+} \quad O^{2-}$$

steps.title: Cross over the values
steps.content:
Using the **Crossover Method**, we take the numerical value of the charge from one ion and move it to become the subscript of the other ion:

- The **2** from the oxygen ion ($O^{2-}$) moves to become the subscript for **sodium**.
- The **1** from the sodium ion ($Na^{1+}$) moves to become the subscript for **oxygen**.

This gives us: $$Na_{2}O_{1}$$

steps.title: Finalize the formula
steps.content:
In chemical formulas, we do not write the subscript "1". Therefore, the formula simplifies to:

$$Na_2O$$

You can verify this is correct because the total charge must be zero:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total charge} &= 2(+1) + 1(-2) \\ &= +2 - 2 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$
