parts.content: Compare the bonding and structure of sodium oxide and magnesium oxide.
parts.markscheme:
- both have giant ionic lattice structures <marks>A1</marks>
- magnesium oxide contains $Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$ ions whereas sodium oxide contains $Na^+$ and $O^{2-}$ ions <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify shared structure
steps.content: To compare these two compounds, we first look at their overall arrangement. Both sodium oxide ($Na_2O$) and magnesium oxide ($MgO$) are ionic compounds. This means they both exist as a **giant ionic lattice** structure, which is a regular, repeating three-dimensional arrangement of oppositely charged ions.

highlights.text: both have giant ionic lattice structures

steps.title: Contrast ionic species
steps.content:
Next, we contrast the specific particles that make up these lattices. 

- In magnesium oxide, the lattice is composed of **$Mg^{2+}$** and **$O^{2-}$** ions.
- In sodium oxide, the lattice is composed of **$Na^{+}$** and **$O^{2-}$** ions.

The main difference in their bonding is that $MgO$ involves ions with higher charges ($2+$ and $2-$) compared to the $1+$ charge of the sodium ion in $Na_2O$. This classification of the specific ions is essential for understanding why their physical properties, like melting points, differ.

highlights.text: magnesium oxide contains Mg2+ and O2- ions whereas sodium oxide contains Na+ and O2- ions

parts.content: Explain why magnesium oxide has a higher melting point than sodium oxide.
parts.markscheme:
- magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) have a higher charge and/or smaller ionic radius than sodium ions ($Na^+$) <marks>M1</marks>
- results in stronger electrostatic attractions between ions, requiring more energy to break the lattice <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the ionic species
steps.content:
To explain the difference in melting points, we first identify the cations in each compound. Sodium oxide contains the sodium ion ($Na^+$), while magnesium oxide contains the magnesium ion ($Mg^{2+}$). Both compounds share the same anion, the oxide ion ($O^{2-}$).

This topic is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**, which explores how ionic properties influence the strength of a crystal lattice.

steps.title: Compare charge and radius
steps.content:
We compare the properties of the $Mg^{2+}$ and $Na^+$ cations:

1.  **Ionic Charge**: The magnesium ion has a higher charge ($+2$) compared to the sodium ion ($+1$).
2.  **Ionic Radius**: $Mg^{2+}$ is smaller than $Na^+$. Although they have the same number of electrons (they are isoelectronic), magnesium has more protons in its nucleus, which pulls the electron shells closer.

In the IB markscheme, identifying either the higher charge or the smaller radius earns the first mark.

steps.title: Evaluate lattice energy
steps.content:
The melting point of an ionic compound depends on its **lattice enthalpy**, which is the energy required to separate the ions. The strength of the electrostatic attraction between ions is governed by Coulomb's Law:

$$F \propto \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

Where $q$ represents the charges and $r$ is the distance between the centers of the ions. Because $Mg^{2+}$ has a higher charge and a smaller radius, the electrostatic attraction between $Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$ is much stronger than between $Na^+$ and $O^{2-}$.

steps.title: Relate to melting point
steps.content:
Because the electrostatic attractions in the magnesium oxide lattice are stronger, more thermal energy is required to overcome these forces and transition the substance from a solid to a liquid state. 

Therefore, magnesium oxide has a significantly higher melting point than sodium oxide.

highlights.text: magnesium oxide has a higher melting point than sodium oxide

parts.content: State one electrical property of these oxides in the solid state and explain the reason for this property.
parts.markscheme:
- they are electrical insulators / do not conduct electricity (in the solid state) <marks>A1</marks>
- ions are held in fixed positions in the lattice and are not mobile <marks>R1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify electrical conductivity
steps.content: In the solid state, ionic compounds such as sodium oxide ($Na_2O$) and magnesium oxide ($MgO$) act as **electrical insulators**. This means they do not conduct electricity. This is a characteristic property of the ionic model covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

highlights.text: they are electrical insulators / do not conduct electricity (in the solid state)

steps.title: Analyze the ionic lattice
steps.content: These oxides form a **giant ionic lattice** where positive metal ions ($Na^+$ or $Mg^{2+}$) and negative oxide ions ($O^{2-}$) are arranged in a regular, repeating 3D pattern. Within this solid structure, the ions are held together by very strong electrostatic forces of attraction.

steps.title: Explain the lack of mobility
steps.content: Electrical conduction requires the presence of mobile charged particles (either electrons or ions) that can move through the material. In the solid state, the ions are **held in fixed positions** within the lattice and are **not mobile**. Since the ions cannot move to carry an electric charge, the solid cannot conduct electricity.

highlights.text: ions are held in fixed positions in the lattice and are not mobile

parts.content: State the number of electrons transferred when calcium reacts with chlorine.
parts.markscheme:
Two electrons (are transferred from calcium to two chlorine atoms) <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify calcium's group
steps.content:
To determine how many electrons are transferred, we first look at the position of calcium ($Ca$) in the periodic table. Calcium is located in **Group 2**. 

This classification is part of **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**, which helps us predict the chemical behavior of elements based on their group number.

steps.title: Determine valence electrons
steps.content:
Using the **Valence Shell Analysis** approach, we know that the group number for main-group elements tells us the number of electrons in the outermost shell. 

Since calcium is in Group 2, it has **2 valence electrons**.

steps.title: Analyze ion formation
steps.content:
Elements react to achieve a stable electron configuration, usually resembling a noble gas (a full outer shell). For a metal like calcium, the easiest way to reach this stability is to lose its valence electrons rather than gaining six more.

When calcium reacts, it loses both of its valence electrons to form a $Ca^{2+}$ ion:

$$Ca \rightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

steps.title: State the electrons transferred
steps.content:
The question asks for the total number of electrons transferred during the reaction with chlorine. Because the calcium atom must lose its 2 valence electrons to become stable, those electrons are moved (transferred) to the chlorine atoms.

Therefore, **two electrons** are transferred.

steps.title: Identify the ionic charges
steps.content:
To understand how electrons move, we first look at the positions of calcium and chlorine in the periodic table to find their preferred ionic charges:

*   **Calcium ($Ca$)**: Located in Group 2, it has 2 valence electrons. It achieves a stable noble gas configuration by losing these 2 electrons, forming a **$Ca^{2+}$** ion.
*   **Chlorine ($Cl$)**: Located in Group 17, it has 7 valence electrons. It needs to gain 1 electron to reach a full outer shell, forming a **$Cl^{-}$** ion.

This is a fundamental concept covered in **S2.1.1: Formation of ions**.

steps.title: Deduce the chemical formula
steps.content:
In an ionic compound like calcium chloride, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge so that the salt is electrically neutral. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Charge of } Ca^{2+} &= +2 \\ \text{Charge of } Cl^{-} &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

To balance the $+2$ charge from one calcium ion, we need two chloride ions ($2 \times -1 = -2$). Therefore, the formula for the salt is **$CaCl_2$**.

steps.title: Determine the electron transfer
steps.content:
Now we look at the transfer that occurred to form those ions. Since the formula is $CaCl_2$, we know that for every one calcium atom that reacts, it must lose enough electrons to reach a $2+$ state.

$$\text{Total electrons lost by } Ca = 2 e^{-}$$

These 2 electrons are transferred directly to the two chlorine atoms (each chlorine atom receiving 1 electron). Thus, the total number of electrons transferred is **2**.

steps.title: Identify initial oxidation state
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the oxidation state of calcium in its starting form. Since the question describes calcium reacting with chlorine, it begins in its elemental state. 

According to the rules for oxidation states, any element in its uncombined state has an oxidation state of $0$. 

This fundamental concept is covered in **S3.1.6 Oxidation states**.

steps.title: Identify final oxidation state
steps.content:
Next, we identify the oxidation state of calcium in the product, **calcium chloride**. 

In the compound $CaCl_{2}$, calcium is a Group 2 metal. In ionic compounds, Group 2 elements consistently exhibit an oxidation state of $+2$ because they lose their two valence electrons to achieve a stable noble gas configuration.

steps.title: Calculate the electron change
steps.content:
Using the **Oxidation State Approach**, we look at the change in oxidation state to determine the number of electrons lost or gained. 

An increase in oxidation state (oxidation) represents a loss of electrons. We calculate the change as follows:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Change} &= \text{Final OS} - \text{Initial OS} \\ &= +2 - 0 \\ &= +2 \end{aligned}$$

A change of $+2$ indicates that each calcium atom has lost 2 electrons during the reaction.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the initial state from the final state.

ti84.instructions: Simply perform the subtraction on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the subtraction into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the final and initial oxidation states.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Because the oxidation state of calcium increases by 2, we conclude that **two electrons** are transferred from each calcium atom to the chlorine atoms to form the salt.

This aligns with the markscheme, which awards the mark for identifying that two electrons are transferred.

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the ions
steps.content: To write the formula for calcium chloride, we first need to identify the individual ions that make up the salt. This involves looking at the periodic table to find the elements' group numbers, which tells us their typical ionic charges. This concept is a core part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

highlights.text: Write the formula of calcium chloride.

steps.title: Determine the calcium charge
steps.content:
Calcium ($\text{Ca}$) is found in **Group 2** of the periodic table. Elements in Group 2 have two valence electrons, which they lose to achieve a stable octet, resulting in a $+2$ charge.

$$\text{Ion: } \text{Ca}^{2+}$$

steps.title: Determine the chloride charge
steps.content:
Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$) is found in **Group 17** (the halogens). These elements have seven valence electrons and need to gain one more to complete their outer shell, resulting in a $-1$ charge.

$$\text{Ion: } \text{Cl}^{-}$$

steps.title: Balance the charges
steps.content:
Ionic compounds must be electrically neutral, meaning the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge. Since we have a $+2$ charge from calcium and only a $-1$ charge from chlorine, we need two chloride ions for every one calcium ion to reach a total charge of zero.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Charge} &= (1 \times +2) + (2 \times -1) \\ &= +2 - 2 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen to check the sum of charges.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the sum of the ion charges.

steps.title: Write the final formula
steps.content:
Now we combine the symbols and use subscripts to indicate the number of each ion. We have one $\text{Ca}$ and two $\text{Cl}$. In chemistry, we don't write the subscript '1'.

$$\text{Final Formula: } \text{CaCl}_2$$

steps.title: Identify the ions
steps.content:
To write the formula for **calcium chloride**, we first identify the elements involved and their positions in the periodic table to determine their ionic charges (Topic S3.1).

- **Calcium (Ca)** is a metal in Group 2, meaning it loses 2 electrons to form a $Ca^{2+}$ ion.
- **Chlorine (Cl)** is a non-metal in Group 17, meaning it gains 1 electron to form a $Cl^-$ ion.

steps.title: Apply the Criss-Cross Method
steps.content:
The **Criss-Cross Method** is a shortcut to ensure the total positive charge equals the total negative charge in an ionic compound. 

We take the numerical value (magnitude) of the charge of each ion and "cross" it over to become the subscript of the opposite ion:

1. Write the symbols and charges: $Ca^{2+}$ and $Cl^{1-}$
2. Move the **2** from the calcium charge to the chlorine subscript.
3. Move the **1** from the chlorine charge to the calcium subscript.

$$\text{Ca}^{\color{red}{2+}} \searrow \text{Cl} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Cl}^{\color{blue}{1-}} \swarrow \text{Ca}$$

steps.title: Verify charge balance
steps.content:
Before finalizing the formula, we can verify that the charges cancel out to zero. If we have 1 Calcium ion and 2 Chloride ions:

$$(1 \times +2) + (2 \times -1) = 0$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to see if it equals zero.

steps.title: Finalize the formula
steps.content:
Based on our criss-cross, we have $Ca_1Cl_2$. 

In chemical formulas, we do not write the subscript '1'. Therefore, the simplest ratio is $\text{CaCl}_2$.

parts.content: State the electron configurations of a sodium atom and a sodium ion.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium atom: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium ion: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the number of electrons
steps.content:
To write the electron configuration, we first look at the Periodic Table to find the atomic number of sodium ($Na$). Sodium is in Group 1 and has an atomic number of 11. 

Since a neutral atom has an equal number of protons and electrons, a neutral sodium atom has **11 electrons** to distribute into subshells.

steps.title: Configure the sodium atom
steps.content:
Following the **Aufbau principle**, we fill the subshells in order of increasing energy ($1s < 2s < 2p < 3s$). We place the 11 electrons as follows:

- $1s$ subshell: 2 electrons
- $2s$ subshell: 2 electrons
- $2p$ subshell: 6 electrons
- $3s$ subshell: 1 electron

Total: $2 + 2 + 6 + 1 = 11$ electrons.

$$\text{Sodium atom: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

steps.title: Determine the ion's electrons
steps.content:
A sodium ion ($Na^+$) has a $1+$ charge. This positive charge means the atom has **lost one electron**. 

To find the number of electrons for the ion:
$$11 - 1 = 10 \text{ electrons}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**, which explains the formation of ions through electron loss or gain.

steps.title: Configure the sodium ion
steps.content:
When sodium forms an ion, it loses its outermost valence electron (from the $3s$ orbital) to achieve a stable noble gas configuration. This leaves us with 10 electrons to place:

- $1s$ subshell: 2 electrons
- $2s$ subshell: 2 electrons
- $2p$ subshell: 6 electrons

$$\text{Sodium ion: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

steps.title: Final Review
steps.content:
In the IB markscheme, you earn 1 mark for each correct configuration. Ensure you write the full subshell notation as requested by the method.

- **Sodium atom:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$ (1 mark)
- **Sodium ion:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ (1 mark)

steps.title: Locate Sodium's atomic number
steps.content: First, we look at the periodic table to find Sodium ($\text{Na}$). Its atomic number is 11, which means a neutral sodium atom has 11 electrons. To understand how these electrons are arranged, we can look at **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: electron configurations of a sodium atom

steps.title: Identify the Noble Gas core
steps.content:
Using the Noble Gas Shorthand method, we find the noble gas that immediately precedes Sodium in the periodic table. That gas is Neon ($\text{Ne}$), which has 10 electrons. 

The electron configuration for Neon is:
$$\text{Ne}: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

In shorthand, we use $[\text{Ne}]$ to represent this filled inner core of 10 electrons.

steps.title: Configure the Sodium atom
steps.content:
Since Sodium has 11 electrons and the Neon core accounts for 10, we have 1 valence electron remaining. This electron enters the next available energy level, which is the $3s$ orbital.

Shorthand version:
$$[\text{Ne}] 3s^1$$

Full version (as required by the markscheme):
$$\text{Na}: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

steps.title: Configure the Sodium ion
steps.content:
Sodium forms a positive ion ($\text{Na}^+$) by losing its single valence electron from the $3s$ orbital. This leaves it with 10 electrons, giving it the same stable configuration as Neon.

Shorthand version:
$$[\text{Ne}]$$

Full version (as required by the markscheme):
$$\text{Na}^+: 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

steps.title: Analyze the sodium ion
steps.content:
To find the electron configurations, we start by looking at the sodium ion ($Na^{+}$). Sodium has an atomic number of 11, meaning a neutral atom has 11 electrons. To form a $1+$ ion, it loses one electron.

$$11 - 1 = 10\text{ electrons}$$

steps.title: Identify isoelectronic noble gas
steps.content:
A species with 10 electrons is **isoelectronic** with the noble gas neon ($Ne$). This means they share the same electron configuration. Using the Aufbau principle, we fill the orbitals for 10 electrons:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$$

This is the configuration for the sodium ion.

steps.title: Determine sodium atom configuration
steps.content:
Now we work toward the neutral sodium atom. Since the neutral atom has 11 electrons (one more than the ion), we simply add the next electron into the next available energy sublevel. 

According to the order of orbital filling (which you can review in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**), the next orbital after $2p$ is $3s$. Adding one electron to the $Na^{+}$ core gives us:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$$

steps.title: Final check and marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn 1 mark for each correct configuration. Ensure you write out the full subshell notation as requested:

- **Sodium atom:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$
- **Sodium ion:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$

parts.content: Deduce the formula of the ionic compound formed between sodium and oxygen.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{Na_2O}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify ion charges
steps.content:
To deduce the formula using the charge balancing method, we first need to identify the charges of the sodium and oxygen ions. We can find these by looking at their positions in the Periodic Table:

*   **Sodium (Na)** is in **Group 1**. Elements in Group 1 lose one electron to form a stable outer shell, resulting in a **$1+$** charge: $\text{Na}^{+}$.
*   **Oxygen (O)** is in **Group 16**. Elements in Group 16 gain two electrons to complete their outer shell, resulting in a **$2-$** charge: $\text{O}^{2-}$.

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** and **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**.

steps.title: Balance the total charge
steps.content:
Ionic compounds must be electrically neutral, meaning the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge (the net charge is zero).

Let's set up an equation where $x$ is the number of sodium ions and $y$ is the number of oxygen ions:

$$x(+1) + y(-2) = 0$$

To find the simplest ratio, if we have one oxygen ion ($y = 1$):

$$x(1) - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$$

This tells us we need **two** $\text{Na}^{+}$ ions for every **one** $\text{O}^{2-}$ ion to balance the charges.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the net charge of the ions.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the charges.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the net charge.

calculator.description: Verify the charge balance for 2 sodium ions and 1 oxygen ion.

steps.title: Write the chemical formula
steps.content:
Now that we know the ratio is $2:1$, we write the formula by placing the number of ions as subscripts after the element symbols. 

Note that in IB Chemistry, the subscript '1' is never written; it is implied if no number is present. 

Therefore, the formula is:
$$\text{Na}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Identify sodium's valence electrons
steps.content: To determine how sodium bonds, we first look at its position in the periodic table. Sodium ($\text{Na}$) is in **Group 1**, which means it has **1 valence electron**. According to the octet rule, sodium will try to lose this single electron to achieve a stable, full outer shell.

steps.title: Identify oxygen's valence electrons
steps.content:
Next, we look at oxygen ($\text{O}$), which is in **Group 16**. Atoms in this group have **6 valence electrons**. 

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** and **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**. Knowing the group number helps you quickly find the number of valence electrons!

steps.title: Calculate oxygen's electron requirement
steps.content:
The **octet rule** states that main-group elements tend to combine in such a way that each atom has eight electrons in its valence shell. Since oxygen already has 6 valence electrons, it needs 2 more to complete its octet:

$$\text{Electrons needed} = 8 - 6 = 2$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 8 minus 6.

ti84.instructions: Enter 8 - 6 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 8 - 6 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verification of electrons needed for oxygen's octet.

steps.title: Balance the electron transfer
steps.content:
Each sodium atom can provide only **1 electron** when it becomes an ion ($\text{Na}^{+}$). Because oxygen requires **2 electrons** to become a stable oxide ion ($\text{O}^{2-}$), we must use two sodium atoms to satisfy one oxygen atom.

$$\text{Number of Na atoms} = \frac{2 \text{ electrons needed}}{1 \text{ electron per Na}} = 2$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.

ti84.instructions: Divide 2 by 1.

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2/1.

calculator.description: Calculation of the number of sodium atoms required.

steps.title: Deduce the final formula
steps.content:
Since it takes two sodium atoms for every one oxygen atom, we write the chemical formula by placing a subscript '2' after the symbol for sodium. 

The final formula is **$\text{Na}_2\text{O}$**.

In the IB exam, you earn the mark by correctly identifying this ratio and writing the formula as shown in the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify the ion charges
steps.content:
To use the criss-cross method, we first need to determine the charges of the ions based on their positions in the periodic table (Topic S2.1.1).

*   **Sodium (Na)** is in Group 1, so it forms a cation with a $1+$ charge: $\text{Na}^{+}$
*   **Oxygen (O)** is in Group 16, so it forms an anion with a $2-$ charge: $\text{O}^{2-}$

steps.title: Apply the criss-cross shortcut
steps.content:
Now, we take the numerical value of each charge and "cross" it over to become the subscript of the opposite ion. This ensures the total positive and negative charges cancel out to zero.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{Na}^{\mathbf{1+}} \quad \text{O}^{\mathbf{2-}} \\ &\quad \searrow \quad \swarrow \\ &\text{Na}_{\mathbf{2}} \quad \text{O}_{\mathbf{1}} \end{aligned}$$

The number $2$ from the oxygen charge becomes the subscript for sodium, and the number $1$ from the sodium charge becomes the subscript for oxygen.

steps.title: Write the final formula
steps.content:
In chemistry, we don't write the subscript "1" in the final formula. Combining the elements gives us:

$$\text{Na}_2\text{O}$$

This confirms that two sodium ions ($2 \times +1 = +2$) are needed to balance one oxide ion ($-2$) for a neutral compound.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: Sodium oxide is composed of a metal (sodium) and a non-metal (oxygen). When these elements react, electrons are transferred from the metal to the non-metal, resulting in the formation of an ionic compound. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Specify the ions formed
steps.content: In this compound, sodium atoms lose one electron to become $\text{Na}^+$ ions, while oxygen atoms gain two electrons to become $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. The chemical formula $\text{Na}_2\text{O}$ reflects the $2:1$ ratio needed for the compound to be electrically neutral.

steps.title: Describe the lattice structure
steps.content:
Instead of forming discrete molecules, these ions arrange themselves into a regular, repeating three-dimensional arrangement. This structure is known as a **giant ionic lattice**.

Award 1 mark for identifying the structure as a giant ionic lattice of $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions.

steps.title: Explain the bonding forces
steps.content:
The bonding in sodium oxide is the **strong electrostatic attraction** between the oppositely charged $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. This attraction acts in all directions throughout the lattice, giving the compound its high melting point.

Award 1 mark for describing the strong electrostatic forces between oppositely charged ions.

parts.content: State the charge on the magnesium and oxide ions in $\mathrm{MgO}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Locate Magnesium's group
steps.content:
First, we look at the periodic table to find Magnesium ($\text{Mg}$). You will find it in **Group 2**. 

This is a key part of **Topic 3.1: The periodic table**, where we learn that the group number tells us about the valence (outer shell) electrons.

steps.title: Determine Magnesium's charge
steps.content:
Elements in Group 2 have **2 valence electrons**. To reach a stable, full outer shell (like a noble gas), metals like magnesium will lose these electrons.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Neutral Mg} &\rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^- \end{aligned}$$

Because it loses two negatively charged electrons, the magnesium ion has a charge of **$2+$**.

highlights.text: charge on the magnesium and oxide ions

steps.title: Locate Oxygen's group
steps.content:
Next, find Oxygen ($\text{O}$) on the periodic table. It is located in **Group 16**. 

Elements in Group 16 have **6 valence electrons** in their outer shell.

steps.title: Determine Oxygen's charge
steps.content:
To achieve a stable octet (8 electrons), non-metals like oxygen prefer to gain electrons. Since it has 6, it needs to gain **2 more** to reach 8.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{O} + 2e^- &\rightarrow \text{O}^{2-} \end{aligned}$$

By gaining two negatively charged electrons, the oxygen atom becomes an oxide ion with a charge of **$2-$**.

highlights.text: charge on the magnesium and oxide ions

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
In the compound $\text{MgO}$, the charges must balance out ($+2$ and $-2$ sum to zero). The ions present are:

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ and } \text{O}^{2-}$$

steps.title: Assign oxygen's oxidation state
steps.content: According to the standard rules for oxidation states, oxygen is assigned an oxidation state of $-2$ in most compounds, including metal oxides like $\mathrm{MgO}$. This reflects the fact that oxygen usually gains two electrons to achieve a full outer shell, forming the oxide ion.

steps.title: Apply the neutrality rule
steps.content:
For a neutral compound, the sum of the oxidation states of all the atoms must equal zero. We can set up a simple algebraic equation where $x$ represents the oxidation state of magnesium:

$$\text{Oxidation state of Mg} + \text{Oxidation state of O} = 0$$

Substituting the known value for oxygen:

$$x + (-2) = 0$$

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the calculation menu and add 2 to 0.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, solve for x in the equation x - 2 = 0 by adding 2 to both sides.

desmos.instructions: Solve for x in the expression x - 2 = 0.

calculator.description: Verify the simple algebraic calculation to ensure the sum equals zero.

steps.title: Determine magnesium's charge
steps.content:
Solving the equation $$x - 2 = 0$$ gives us $$x = +2$$. This means the oxidation state of magnesium is $+2$. 

In IB Chemistry, while oxidation states are written with the sign first ($+2$), the charge on an ion is written with the number first ($2+$).

steps.title: Finalize the ion charges
steps.content:
Based on the oxidation states calculated, we can identify the specific ions present in the lattice. This concept is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**.

- The magnesium ion has a charge of $2+$, written as $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$.
- The oxide ion has a charge of $2-$, written as $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$.

steps.title: Identify magnesium's ionic charge
steps.content:
First, we look at the position of magnesium ($Mg$) in the periodic table. Magnesium is located in **Group 2**. 

To achieve a stable electron configuration (a full outer shell), metals in Group 2 lose their two valence electrons. This results in a cation with a charge of $+2$:

$$Mg^{2+}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**, which explains the formation of ions based on group trends.

steps.title: Identify oxygen's ionic charge
steps.content:
Next, we find oxygen ($O$) in the periodic table. Oxygen is in **Group 16** (sometimes referred to as Group 6). 

Non-metals in this group have six valence electrons and need to gain two more to complete their outer shell. By gaining two negatively charged electrons, oxygen forms an anion with a charge of $-2$:

$$O^{2-}$$

steps.title: Balance the charges
steps.content:
In an ionic compound, the total positive charge must equal the total negative charge so that the compound is electrically neutral.

We have:
- Magnesium ion: $+2$
- Oxide ion: $-2$

Since $(+2) + (-2) = 0$, the charges are already balanced in a $1:1$ ratio. We only need one magnesium ion for every one oxide ion.

steps.title: Write the final formula
steps.content:
Using the $1:1$ ratio determined in the previous step, we write the symbols together. By convention, we write the metal (cation) first, followed by the non-metal (anion). 

We do not write the number '1' in chemical formulas, so the final formula is:

$$\text{MgO}$$

This correctly represents the binary compound magnesium oxide as required by the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To determine how electrons are transferred, we first look at the positions of Magnesium (Mg) and Oxygen (O) in the periodic table to find their valence electrons:

*   **Magnesium (Mg)** is in **Group 2**, so it has **2 valence electrons**.
*   **Oxygen (O)** is in **Group 16**, so it has **6 valence electrons**.

This concept is covered in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**, where you learn that an element's group number tells us about its outer-shell electrons.

steps.title: Determine stable configurations
steps.content:
Atoms generally react to achieve a full outer shell (an octet), which is a lower-energy, stable state. 

*   **Magnesium** will **lose 2 electrons** to reveal the full shell underneath. This forms a $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ion.
*   **Oxygen** will **gain 2 electrons** to complete its outer shell (reaching 8). This forms an $\text{O}^{2-}$ ion.

steps.title: Balance electron transfer
steps.content:
In an ionic compound, the total number of electrons lost by the metal must equal the total number of electrons gained by the non-metal. This ensures the resulting compound is electrically neutral.

$$\text{Electrons lost by Mg} = 2$$
$$\text{Electrons gained by O} = 2$$

Since one Magnesium atom provides exactly the number of electrons one Oxygen atom needs, they combine in a **1:1 ratio**.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: To understand why magnesium oxide ($MgO$) has a high melting point, we first need to identify the type of bonding present. Since magnesium is a metal and oxygen is a non-metal, they form an **ionic compound**. This topic is a key part of **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Describe the electrostatic forces
steps.content: In the solid state, $MgO$ exists as a **giant ionic lattice**. This structure is held together by the **strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions** ($Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$). This is the first key point that earns a mark in IB exams.

highlights.text: Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions

steps.title: Link forces to energy
steps.content: Melting involves providing enough thermal energy to overcome the attractive forces between particles. Because the electrostatic forces in $MgO$ are so strong, a **large amount of energy is required to break these forces** and allow the ions to move out of their fixed positions in the lattice. This high energy requirement results in a high melting point.

highlights.text: Requires a large amount of energy to break these forces

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: Magnesium oxide ($MgO$) is an **ionic compound**. In the IB curriculum, specifically in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**, we learn that the physical properties of these substances—like melting point—are determined by the strength of the lattice structure held together by ionic bonds.

highlights.text: Magnesium oxide is a white crystalline solid

steps.title: Analyze ion charge magnitudes
steps.content:
The strength of an ionic bond is directly related to the magnitude of the charges on the ions. In $MgO$:

- Magnesium forms a $Mg^{2+}$ ion.
- Oxygen forms an $O^{2-}$ ion.

Compared to a compound like sodium chloride ($Na^{+}$ and $Cl^{-}$), the charges here are much higher. In chemistry, we often look at the product of the charges ($2 \times 2 = 4$ vs. $1 \times 1 = 1$) to understand the relative strength of the attraction.

steps.title: Relate charge to attraction
steps.content:
Because the $Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$ ions have high charge magnitudes, there is a **strong electrostatic attraction** between the oppositely charged ions throughout the giant ionic lattice.

This earns you the first mark by identifying the nature and strength of the force.

highlights.text: Strong electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions

steps.title: Connect energy to melting
steps.content:
To melt the solid, you must provide enough thermal energy to overcome these attractions. Because the electrostatic forces in $MgO$ are so strong due to the $2+$ and $2-$ charges, it **requires a large amount of energy to break these forces**.

This explains why the melting point is exceptionally high (about $2852^{\circ}C$!) and secures the second mark.

highlights.text: Requires a large amount of energy to break these forces

steps.title: Locate data points
steps.content:
To determine the trend, we first identify the specific data points on the graph for the substances mentioned. 

*   $\mathrm{NaCl}$ corresponds to the metal in **Period 3**.
*   $\mathrm{RbCl}$ corresponds to the metal in **Period 5**.

Looking at the horizontal axis (Period Number), find the points at 3 and 5.

This aligns with the general trend for Group 1 halides: as the metal cation increases in size (moving down the group), the lattice energy usually decreases, leading to lower melting points. This is a key concept in **Topic S3.1.3: Periodicity of properties**.

methods.label: Ion Size Similarity and Lattice Efficiency

steps.title: Compare ionic radii
steps.content:
To understand the difference in melting points, we first look at the sizes of the cations. Sodium ($Na$) is in Period 3, while Lithium ($Li$) is in Period 2. 

Because $Na^+$ has an additional electron shell compared to $Li^+$, the $Na^+$ ion has a larger ionic radius. In both compounds, the anion is the relatively large $Cl^-$ ion.

highlights.text: Na+ has a larger ionic radius than Li+

steps.title: Explain ion size similarity
steps.content:
A key factor in lattice stability is the **size similarity** between the cation and the anion. 

In $NaCl$, the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are more similar in size than the $Li^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. This similarity allows the ions to pack together more effectively in the crystal lattice. 

This efficient packing leads to a more stable arrangement with stronger overall electrostatic attractions, resulting in a higher melting point for $NaCl$.

highlights.text: NaCl forms a more stable lattice because Na+ and Cl- are more similar in size

steps.title: Account for LiCl's anomaly
steps.content:
In $LiCl$, there is a significant size mismatch between the very small $Li^+$ and the large $Cl^-$. This mismatch leads to two effects:
1. **Inefficient Packing:** The large $Cl^-$ ions may touch each other (anion-anion repulsion), preventing the $Li^+$ and $Cl^-$ from getting close enough for maximum attraction.
2. **Polarization:** The high charge density of the tiny $Li^+$ ion polarizes the large $Cl^-$ electron cloud. This introduces a degree of **covalent character** into the bond, which weakens the ionic lattice and lowers the melting point compared to a purely ionic model.

highlights.text: small Li+ ion polarizes Cl- slightly, weakening the ionic character

steps.title: Compare ionic sizes
steps.content:
First, we look at the positions of the metals in the periodic table. Lithium ($\text{Li}$) is in Period 2 and Sodium ($\text{Na}$) is in Period 3. 

As we move down Group 1, the number of occupied electron shells increases, so the ionic radius also increases. Therefore, the $\text{Li}^+$ ion is significantly smaller than the $\text{Na}^+$ ion.

steps.title: Explain charge density
steps.content: Both ions have a charge of $+1$, but because the $\text{Li}^+$ ion is much smaller, its charge is concentrated into a smaller volume. This gives the $\text{Li}^+$ ion a much higher **charge density** compared to the $\text{Na}^+$ ion.

steps.title: Describe polarization
steps.content:
Due to its high charge density, the small $\text{Li}^+$ ion exerts a strong attractive force on the electron cloud of the large $\text{Cl}^-$ ion. 

This causes the electron cloud of the chloride ion to distort or "pull" toward the lithium ion. This process is known as **polarization**.

highlights.text: small Li+ ion polarizes Cl- slightly

steps.title: Link to covalent character
steps.content:
As the electron cloud of $\text{Cl}^-$ is pulled toward $\text{Li}^+$, the electrons are partially shared between the two atoms. This introduces a degree of **covalent character** into the bond. 

In the **bonding continuum** (Topic S2.4), we recognize that no bond is $100\%$ ionic or $100\%$ covalent. The significant covalent character in $\text{LiCl}$ makes the lattice less stable than a purely ionic model would predict, leading to a lower melting point.

highlights.text: weakening the ionic character and lowering melting point

steps.title: Consider NaCl lattice stability
steps.content:
In contrast, the $\text{Na}^+$ ion is larger and has a lower charge density, so it does not polarize the $\text{Cl}^-$ ion as effectively. 

Furthermore, because $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ are more similar in size than $\text{Li}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$, they can pack together more efficiently in a crystal lattice. This creates a more stable lattice with stronger overall electrostatic attractions, resulting in the higher melting point of $\text{NaCl}$.

highlights.text: Na+ and Cl- are more similar in size, allowing stronger electrostatic attraction

steps.title: Analyze the chemical composition
steps.content:
To identify the bonding, let's look at the elements involved in these compounds. The question specifies that these are **chlorides of Group 1 metals**. 

*   **Group 1 metals** (such as $Li$, $Na$, $K$) have one electron in their outer shell.
*   **Chlorine** is a non-metal in Group 17 with seven electrons in its outer shell.

This is a classic combination of a reactive metal and a reactive non-metal, which is a key indicator of the type of bonding present.

steps.title: Explain electron transfer
steps.content:
When a metal from Group 1 reacts with a non-metal like chlorine, a transfer of electrons occurs to achieve stable noble gas configurations:

1.  The metal atom loses its one valence electron to form a positive ion ($M^+$).
2.  The chlorine atom gains that electron to form a negative chloride ion ($Cl^-$).

This process is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** of your syllabus. The resulting electrostatic attraction between these oppositely charged ions is the definition of the bond we are looking for.

steps.title: Correlate with physical properties
steps.content:
We can confirm our reasoning by looking at the provided graph. The melting points for all these compounds are quite high (ranging roughly between $600^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $800^{\circ}\text{C}$).

High melting points are characteristic of substances with a **giant lattice structure**, where strong electrostatic forces must be overcome to change the state from solid to liquid. This physical evidence strongly supports the conclusion that the bonding is ionic.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: Based on the combination of a Group 1 metal and a non-metal, and supported by the high melting points shown in the graph, the bonding present in all these compounds is **ionic bonding**.

steps.title: Analyze the graph data
steps.content: By looking at the provided graph, we can see that the melting points for all the Group 1 chlorides are consistently high, falling within the range of approximately $800\text{ K}$ to $1100\text{ K}$.

highlights.text: melting points of the chlorides of group 1 metals

steps.title: Link temperature to structure
steps.content:
High melting points indicate that a large amount of energy is required to break the bonds and change the state from solid to liquid. This suggests that these compounds exist in a **giant lattice structure** rather than as simple molecules. 

You can learn more about these arrangements in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**, which covers how lattice structures influence physical properties.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content:
A giant lattice structure held together by strong electrostatic forces of attraction between oppositely charged ions (metals from Group 1 and non-metal chlorine) is characteristic of **ionic bonding**.

According to the markscheme, the correct answer is:
- **Ionic bonding**

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.content: The question asks for the type of bonding in the chlorides of Group 1 metals (such as $\text{LiCl}$, $\text{NaCl}$, $\text{KCl}$, etc.). These compounds consist of a metal from the left side of the periodic table and a non-metal (chlorine) from the right side. To determine the bonding type using the **Electronegativity Comparison** method, we first need to look up the electronegativity ($\chi$) values in the IB Data Booklet.

highlights.text: Identify the type of bonding present in all these compounds.

steps.title: Find electronegativity values
steps.content:
Using the IB Data Booklet (typically Section 8), we can find the Pauling electronegativity values for the elements involved:

- Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$): $3.2$
- Lithium ($\text{Li}$): $1.0$
- Sodium ($\text{Na}$): $0.9$
- Potassium ($\text{K}$): $0.8$
- Rubidium ($\text{Rb}$): $0.8$
- Cesium ($\text{Cs}$): $0.7$

Notice that the metals have very low electronegativities while chlorine has a high electronegativity.

steps.title: Calculate electronegativity difference
steps.content:
The bond type is determined by the difference in electronegativity ($\Delta\chi$) between the two atoms. We calculate this by subtracting the smaller value from the larger value:

$$\Delta\chi = \chi(\text{Cl}) - \chi(\text{Metal})$$

For $\text{LiCl}$:
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 1.0 = 2.2$$

For $\text{NaCl}$:
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 0.9 = 2.3$$

steps.title: Compare to ionic threshold
steps.content:
In the IB curriculum (Topic **S2.4: From models to materials**), a bonding difference ($\Delta\chi$) greater than approximately $1.8$ is generally considered to indicate **ionic bonding**.

Since all the calculated values for Group 1 chlorides (ranging from $2.2$ to $2.5$) are significantly greater than $1.8$, we conclude that the bonding is ionic. This is supported by the high melting points shown in the graph, which are characteristic of strong electrostatic attractions in an ionic lattice.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The bonding present in all these compounds is **ionic bonding**.

steps.title: Analyze the cation size trend
steps.content:
As we move down Group 1 from $\mathrm{Na}^+$ to $\mathrm{Rb}^+$, the period number increases. This means the metal atoms have more occupied electron shells, leading to an increase in the **ionic radius** of the cation. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic 3.1.2: Electron configuration and group trends**.

steps.title: Evaluate inter-ionic distance
steps.content: In an ionic lattice, the distance between the ions is the sum of their radii. Since the chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^-$) is the same for all these salts, the increase in the cation's radius directly results in an **increased distance** between the nuclei of the oppositely charged ions in the crystal lattice.

steps.title: Relate attraction to melting point
steps.content:
The strength of the ionic bond depends on the **electrostatic attraction** between the ions. According to the principles of **Topic 2.1.3: Lattice structures and properties**, this attraction is weaker when the distance between the ions is greater. 

Because the attraction is weaker in $\mathrm{RbCl}$ than in $\mathrm{NaCl}$, less thermal energy is needed to overcome the lattice forces, which results in a lower melting point.

steps.title: Identify the trend in size
steps.content:
As we move from $\mathrm{NaCl}$ to $\mathrm{RbCl}$, we are moving down Group 1 of the periodic table. As the period number increases, the number of occupied electron shells in the metal cation increases. 

Therefore, the **ionic radius** of the cation increases from $\mathrm{Na}^+$ to $\mathrm{Rb}^+$.

steps.title: Analyze charge density
steps.content:
All Group 1 cations ($\mathrm{Na}^+$, $\mathrm{K}^+$, $\mathrm{Rb}^+$) carry a constant charge of $+1$. 

Since the charge remains the same but the ionic radius increases, the **charge density** (charge per unit volume) of the cation decreases as we go down the group. This is a key concept in understanding the strength of ionic lattices, which you can review in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Relate to lattice enthalpy
steps.content:
The melting point of an ionic compound depends on the strength of the electrostatic attraction between its ions, often measured by its **lattice enthalpy**. 

A larger ionic radius means the centers of the positive and negative ions are further apart. Because the attraction is weaker at greater distances (and lower charge densities), the lattice enthalpy decreases from $\mathrm{NaCl}$ to $\mathrm{RbCl}$.

steps.title: Explain the melting point
steps.content:
Because the electrostatic attractions in the lattice become weaker, less thermal energy is required to overcome these forces and transition the solid into a liquid state.

This explains why the **melting points decrease** as the cations become larger and the lattice enthalpy becomes less exothermic.

parts.content: Write the electron configuration of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$.

steps.title: Identify neutral Copper's electrons
steps.content: To find the configuration of the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, we first look at the neutral copper atom. From the periodic table, copper has an atomic number of 29, meaning it has 29 electrons.

steps.title: Apply the copper exception
steps.content:
According to the Aufbau principle, we might expect the configuration to end in $4s^2 3d^9$. However, copper is a well-known exception in the IB syllabus. To achieve a more stable, full $3d$ sublevel, one electron promotes from the $4s$ to the $3d$ orbital.

Neutral Cu configuration:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^{10}$$

steps.title: Remove the $4s$ electron
steps.content:
When transition metals form ions, a critical rule to remember is that electrons are always removed from the **$4s$ orbital before the $3d$ orbital**. 

To form the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, we need to remove one electron from the neutral atom configuration we just wrote.

steps.title: Determine final configuration
steps.content:
Removing the single electron from the $4s^1$ subshell leaves us with a full $3d^{10}$ subshell. 

$$\text{Cu}^{+} \text{ configuration: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^{10}$$

steps.title: Determine number of electrons
steps.content:
To find the electron configuration of the ion, we first look at the atomic number of Copper ($Z = 29$). 

The $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion has a positive charge, meaning it has lost one electron. We calculate the total number of electrons remaining:

$$29 - 1 = 28 \text{ electrons}$$

steps.title: Apply transition metal rule
steps.content:
For transition metal ions, there is a specific rule: electrons are always removed from the $4s$ subshell before the $3d$ subshell. 

Because we are forming a $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, the $4s$ subshell will be empty ($4s^0$). This is a key concept in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** regarding how d-block elements behave.

steps.title: Fill the orbitals
steps.content:
Now, we distribute the 28 electrons into the subshells in order of increasing energy, ensuring the $4s$ orbital is left empty:

1.  $1s^2$ (2 electrons)
2.  $2s^2$ (4 total)
3.  $2p^6$ (10 total)
4.  $3s^2$ (12 total)
5.  $3p^6$ (18 total)
6.  $3d^{10}$ (28 total)

Note that the $3d$ subshell is filled to its maximum capacity of 10 electrons to reach the total count of 28.

steps.title: State the final configuration
steps.content:
The full electron configuration for the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^{10}$$

(Note: You do not need to include $4s^0$ in your final answer unless specifically asked).

parts.content: Explain why there is a relatively large difference between the first and second ionization energies of copper.
parts.markscheme:
- The second ionization energy removes electron from stable/closed ${3d^{10}}$ configuration, requiring much more energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The first ionization energy removes electron from 4s subshell where electron is relatively shielded <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To explain the large jump in energy between the first and second ionization energies (IE), we need to look at which subshells the electrons are being removed from. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

highlights.text: relatively large difference between the first and second ionization energies

steps.title: Write Copper's electron configuration
steps.content:
First, let's write the electron configuration for a neutral copper atom ($Z = 29$). Copper is a well-known exception to the Aufbau principle because a full $3d$ subshell is particularly stable:

$$\text{Cu: } [Ar] \, 3d^{10} 4s^1$$

When atoms form ions, the electrons in the highest main energy level (the $4s$ orbital) are removed first.

steps.title: Analyze the first ionization
steps.content:
The **first ionization energy** ($IE_1$) corresponds to removing the single electron from the $4s$ subshell:

$$\text{Cu}(g) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^+(g) + e^- \quad \text{Config of Cu}^+: [Ar] \, 3d^{10}$$

This electron is relatively far from the nucleus and is shielded by the inner $3d$ electrons, so it requires a comparatively low amount of energy to remove.

steps.title: Analyze the second ionization
steps.content:
The **second ionization energy** ($IE_2$) involves removing an electron from the $\text{Cu}^+$ ion:

$$\text{Cu}^+(g) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(g) + e^- \quad \text{Config of Cu}^{2+}: [Ar] \, 3d^9$$

This electron must be removed from a **stable, full $3d^{10}$ subshell**. Because this subshell is closer to the nucleus and is already "closed" (full), it is much more difficult to remove an electron, leading to a much higher energy requirement.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam marks:
1. The first electron is removed from the **$4s$ subshell**, which is shielded and further from the nucleus.
2. The second electron is removed from a **stable $3d^{10}$ configuration**, which requires significantly more energy to break.

steps.title: Identify the electron configuration
steps.content:
To understand ionization energy, we first need to look at the electron configuration of a neutral copper atom ($Cu$). 

Copper is an exception to the general filling rules. Instead of $[Ar] 4s^2 3d^9$, it has a full $d$-subshell for extra stability:

$$[Ar] 3d^{10} 4s^1$$

This tells us that the first electron removed will come from the $4s$ orbital, and the second will come from the $3d$ orbital.

steps.title: Analyze the first ionization
steps.content:
The **first ionization energy** involves removing the single electron from the $4s$ subshell. 

In terms of **shielding and energy levels**:
- The $4s$ electron is in the $n=4$ main energy level, which is the furthest from the nucleus.
- It is highly shielded from the positive pull of the nucleus by the 18 electrons in the inner shells ($n=1, 2, \text{ and } 3$).

Because it is far away and well-shielded, it requires relatively little energy to remove.

highlights.text: The first ionization energy removes electron from 4s subshell where electron is relatively shielded

steps.title: Analyze the second ionization
steps.content:
The **second ionization energy** involves removing an electron from the $Cu^+$ ion, which has the configuration $[Ar] 3d^{10}$.

Now, the electron must be removed from the $3d$ subshell ($n=3$ level):
- The $n=3$ level is closer to the nucleus than the $n=4$ level.
- Once the $4s$ electron is gone, the remaining electrons experience a significantly higher **effective nuclear charge** because there is less shielding from outer electrons.
- Furthermore, the $3d^{10}$ configuration is a **stable, closed subshell**.

highlights.text: The second ionization energy removes electron from stable/closed 3d^{10} configuration, requiring much more energy

steps.title: Conclude the energy difference
steps.content:
The large difference arises because we are moving from removing a weakly-held, highly-shielded electron in a higher energy level ($n=4$) to removing an electron from a lower, more stable energy level ($n=3$) that is closer to the nucleus and less shielded.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3.7: Successive ionization energies**. Understanding how $n$ levels and shielding affect the pull of the nucleus is key to predicting these jumps!

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Let oxidation state of O = -2 <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Total charge must equal 0: $2x + (-2) = 0$ where x is oxidation state of Cu

- Therefore oxidation state of Cu = +1

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer of +1 with or without working</note>

steps.title: Identify oxygen's oxidation state
steps.content:
To find the oxidation state of copper, we start with the known rules. In most compounds, oxygen has an oxidation state of $-2$. This is a fundamental concept found in **Topic 3.1: The periodic table: Classification of elements** (S3.1.6).

Let the oxidation state of oxygen be $-2$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
A key rule for neutral compounds is that the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms must equal $0$. 

Let $x$ represent the oxidation state of copper. Since there are two copper atoms and one oxygen atom in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, we can set up the following algebraic equation:

$$2(x) + 1(-2) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for copper
steps.content:
Now, we solve for $x$ to find the oxidation state of a single copper atom:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x - 2 &= 0 \\ 2x &= 2 \\ x &= +1 \end{aligned}$$

In oxidation states, it is important to always include the sign (even for positive values).

gdc.instructions: Use the equation solver mode on your calculator. Input the equation 2x - 2 = 0 and solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Go to the 'Numeric Solver' by pressing [MATH] and then [0] (Solver...). Enter the equation: 0 = 2x - 2 and press [OK] (Graph), then [SOLVE] (Graph).

desmos.instructions: Plot the line y = 2x - 2 and find where it intersects the x-axis (where y = 0).

calculator.description: Solving the simple linear equation 2x - 2 = 0

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The oxidation state of copper in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ is $+1$. 

This makes sense as copper is a transition metal that can exhibit multiple oxidation states, and in this specific oxide, it exists as the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion.

steps.title: Identify the Oxide Ion
steps.content:
To find the oxidation state using the ionic charge balancing method, we first identify the charge of the known ion. Oxygen almost always forms the oxide ion, $O^{2-}$, when combined with metals. This is because oxygen belongs to Group 16 and gains two electrons to reach a stable configuration.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**.

steps.title: Set Up Charge Balance
steps.content:
A neutral compound like $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ has a total net charge of zero. Based on the chemical formula, we have two copper ions for every one oxide ion. We can represent the unknown oxidation state of copper as $x$.

According to the principle of electrical neutrality:

$$\text{Total Charge} = (2 \times \text{charge of Cu}) + (1 \times \text{charge of O}) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for Copper's Charge
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the charge of the oxide ion ($-2$) into our equation and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + (-2) &= 0 \\ 2x &= +2 \\ x &= +1 \end{aligned}$$

Each copper ion must have a charge of $+1$ to balance the $-2$ charge from the single oxygen atom.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 / 2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 divided by 2.

desmos.instructions: Type 2/2.

calculator.description: Calculate the charge per copper atom by dividing the total positive charge required by the number of copper atoms.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ is **+1**.

In the IB markscheme, you earn the mark by identifying that the sum of charges must be zero and correctly calculating the value. Note that oxidation states must always be written with the sign first (e.g., $+1$ rather than $1+$).

steps.title: Identify the IUPAC name
steps.content: To find the oxidation state using the nomenclature approach, we look directly at the name of the compound provided in the question: **Copper(I) oxide**.

steps.title: Interpret the Roman numeral
steps.content:
In the IUPAC naming system, transition metals (like copper) can have multiple possible oxidation states. To specify which one is present in a compound, a Roman numeral is written in parentheses immediately after the metal's name. 

This numeral indicates the **oxidation state** of the metal ion.

steps.title: Determine the value
steps.content:
The name contains the Roman numeral **(I)**. In chemistry notation, this corresponds to a value of $+1$. 

This is a quick and effective way to identify oxidation states without needing to perform a full algebraic calculation using the chemical formula $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Therefore, the oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $+1$. 

As noted in the markscheme, you receive full credit for stating the correct oxidation state, whether or not you show the algebraic working.

parts.content: Describe the type of bonding present in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$, and explain how this type of bonding affects its physical properties.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ exhibits ionic bonding between $\mathrm{Cu}^+$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Ionic bonding results in high melting/boiling points and makes it a good electrical insulator in solid state (as ions are fixed in place) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, we look at the elements involved. Copper ($\text{Cu}$) is a metal and Oxygen ($\text{O}$) is a non-metal. 

When a metal reacts with a non-metal, electrons are transferred to form ions. In this compound, we have $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. The bonding is **ionic**, which is defined as the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.

steps.title: Explain high melting point
steps.content: In the **Standard Ionic Model**, these ions are arranged in a regular 3D crystal lattice. Because the electrostatic forces of attraction between the $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions are very strong, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome these bonds. This results in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ having a **high melting point**.

steps.title: Analyze electrical conductivity
steps.content:
For a substance to conduct electricity, it must have mobile charged particles (like electrons or ions). 

In the solid state, the ions in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ are **fixed in place** within the lattice and cannot move to carry a charge. Therefore, solid $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ acts as an **electrical insulator**.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both the specific ions and the link between the structure and properties:

1.  **1 mark**: Identifying the bonding as ionic between $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions.
2.  **1 mark**: Explaining that this leads to a high melting point OR that it is an insulator in the solid state because ions are fixed.

parts.content: Identify the bonding in solid sodium chloride.
parts.markscheme:
Ionic bonding **OR** electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in sodium chloride, we first look at the chemical formula, $\text{NaCl}$. This compound is composed of two different elements:

*   **Sodium** ($\text{Na}$)
*   **Chlorine** ($\text{Cl}$)

steps.title: Classify the elements
steps.content:
Using the periodic table (which you can review in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**), we classify each element based on its position:

*   **Sodium ($\text{Na}$)**: Located in Group 1, it is an alkali **metal**.
*   **Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$)**: Located in Group 17, it is a halogen, which is a **non-metal**.

steps.title: Apply the bonding rule
steps.content:
A fundamental rule in chemistry (covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**) is that when a **metal** reacts with a **non-metal**, the metal atoms lose electrons to the non-metal atoms. 

This electron transfer creates oppositely charged ions ($\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$) that are held together by strong electrostatic forces. This type of bonding is defined as **ionic bonding**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Since sodium is a metal and chlorine is a non-metal, the bonding in solid sodium chloride is ionic.

$$\text{Ionic bonding}$$

steps.title: Find electronegativity values
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type using the **Electronegativity Difference Analysis**, we first need the Pauling electronegativity values ($\chi$) for sodium and chlorine from the IB Data Booklet. 

Looking at the Periodic Table or the table of electronegativities in your Data Booklet:
- Sodium ($Na$): $\chi = 0.9$
- Chlorine ($Cl$): $\chi = 3.2$

steps.title: Calculate the difference
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the absolute difference in electronegativity ($\Delta\chi$) between the two atoms:

$$\Delta\chi = |\chi(Cl) - \chi(Na)|$$
$$\Delta\chi = |3.2 - 0.9| = 2.3$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 3.2 - 0.9 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 in the expression box.

calculator.description: Subtract the electronegativity of sodium from that of chlorine.

steps.title: Apply the ionic threshold
steps.content:
In the IB curriculum (specifically **Topic S2.4: Bonding as a continuum**), we use a threshold for the electronegativity difference to predict the bond character:
- If $\Delta\chi > 1.8$, the bond is considered **primarily ionic**.
- If $\Delta\chi < 1.8$, the bond is considered primarily covalent.

Since our calculated value is $2.3$, which is significantly greater than $1.8$, we can confidently identify the bonding as ionic.

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content:
Based on the electronegativity difference, the bonding in solid sodium chloride is **ionic bonding**.

This represents the strong electrostatic attraction between the positive sodium ions ($Na^+$) and the negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$) arranged in a lattice structure.

highlights.text: Identify the bonding in solid sodium chloride.

parts.content: Deduce an equation to represent the dissolving of one mole of solid sodium chloride in water, including state symbols.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{NaCl(s)} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+\text{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-\text{(aq)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the reactant
steps.content:
The question asks for an equation representing the dissolving of one mole of solid sodium chloride. We begin by writing the chemical formula for the solid salt, including its state symbol.

Reactant: $\text{NaCl(s)}$

steps.title: Identify the aqueous ions
steps.content:
When an ionic compound like sodium chloride dissolves in water, it dissociates into its constituent ions. According to the **ionic model (Topic S2.1)**, the lattice structure breaks apart, and the ions become hydrated. These hydrated ions are represented with the state symbol $\text{(aq)}$ for aqueous.

Sodium chloride consists of:
- Sodium ions: $\text{Na}^+$
- Chloride ions: $\text{Cl}^-$

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Using the **Standard Chemical Equation** approach, we place the solid salt on the left as the reactant and the separated aqueous ions on the right as the products. 

$$\text{NaCl(s)} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+\text{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-\text{(aq)}$$

To earn the mark, ensure that both the charges on the ions and the state symbols are correctly shown.

parts.content: When $8.00\text{ g}$ of sodium chloride is dissolved in $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of water the temperature of the solution decreases from $22.0^\circ\text{C}$ to $20.5^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the heat absorbed by the solution in $\text{J}$.
parts.markscheme:
$Q = mc\Delta T$

$Q = 48.0 \times 4.18 \times 1.5 = 301\text{ (J)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the calorimetry formula
steps.content:
To calculate the heat absorbed during the dissolution of the salt, we use the calorimetry equation from **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**:

$$Q = mcΔT$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the heat change (in $\text{J}$)
- $m$ is the mass of the solution (in $\text{g}$)
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity of the solution (typically $4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$)
- $\u0394T$ is the change in temperature (in $\text{K}$ or $^\circ\text{C}$)

steps.title: Calculate total solution mass
steps.content:
Using the **Total Solution Mass Approach**, we must sum the mass of the water and the mass of the dissolved solute. 

Given that water has a density of $1.00 \text{ g/cm}^3$, the mass of $40.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of water is $40.0 \text{ g}$.

$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{total}} &= m_{\text{water}} + m_{\text{NaCl}} \\ m_{\text{total}} &= 40.0 \text{ g} + 8.00 \text{ g} \\ m_{\text{total}} &= 48.0 \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 40.0 + 8.00 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 40.0 + 8.00 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 40.0 + 8.00 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the total mass of the solution.

steps.title: Determine temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we find the difference between the initial and final temperatures:

$$\begin{aligned} \u0394T &= T_{\text{initial}} - T_{\text{final}} \\ \u0394T &= 22.0 ^\circ\text{C} - 20.5 ^\circ\text{C} \\ \u0394T &= 1.5 ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 22.0 - 20.5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 22.0 - 20.5 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 22.0 - 20.5 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the temperature difference.

steps.title: Calculate heat absorbed
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the heat equation. Note that we use $4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$ as the specific heat capacity for the solution.

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 48.0 \text{ g} \times 4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1} \times 1.5 \text{ K} \\ Q &= 300.96 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the data provided), we get **301 J**.

gdc.instructions: Enter 48.0 * 4.18 * 1.5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 48.0 * 4.18 * 1.5 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 48.0 * 4.18 * 1.5 into a new expression line.

The graph below shows the solubility of different salts in grams per $100\text{ g}$ of water.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/df95827b-0154-4244-8b1b-a82d834dc4ed)

Use the graph to determine the solubility of $\text{NaCl}$ at $90^\circ\text{C}$.
parts.markscheme:
$40\text{ g}/100\text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the target values
steps.content: To solve this, we need to extract information from the graph for a specific salt at a specific temperature. We are looking for the solubility of **$\text{NaCl}$** when the temperature is **$90^\circ\text{C}$**.

steps.title: Locate temperature on x-axis
steps.content:
First, look at the horizontal axis (the x-axis) labeled **Temperature / $^\circ\text{C}$**. 

Find the mark for $90^\circ\text{C}$. On this graph, it is the midpoint between $80$ and $100$.

steps.title: Trace to the NaCl line
steps.content:
From the $90^\circ\text{C}$ mark, move your finger or a ruler vertically upward until you reach the line labeled **$\text{NaCl}$**. 

Notice that the $\text{NaCl}$ line is relatively flat compared to the others, meaning its solubility doesn't change much with temperature.

steps.title: Read the solubility value
steps.content:
Once you hit the $\text{NaCl}$ line at $90^\circ\text{C}$, move horizontally to the left to see where this point aligns with the vertical axis (the y-axis).

The line intersects the y-axis exactly at the value of **$40$**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The vertical axis represents the solubility in grams per $100\text{ g}$ of water. 

Therefore, the solubility of $\text{NaCl}$ at $90^\circ\text{C}$ is:

$$40\text{ g}/100\text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

parts.content: Contrast the effect of temperature on the solubility of potassium chloride, $\text{KCl}$, and cerium(III) sulfate, $\text{Ce}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{KCl}$: solubility increases as temperature increases <marks>A1</marks>

$\text{Ce}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$: solubility decreases as temperature increases <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The question asks us to **contrast** how temperature affects the solubility of two different salts: potassium chloride ($KCl$) and cerium(III) sulfate ($Ce_2(SO_4)_3$). To contrast them, we need to state the specific relationship for each salt.

highlights.text: Contrast the effect of temperature on the solubility of potassium chloride, KCl, and cerium(III) sulfate, Ce2(SO4)3.

steps.title: Trend for Potassium Chloride
steps.content:
For most ionic solids, solubility is an endothermic process. This means that as you increase the temperature, the solubility also increases. 

For **potassium chloride ($KCl$)**, the trend follows this common pattern: **solubility increases as temperature increases**.

steps.title: Trend for Cerium(III) Sulfate
steps.content:
While most salts become more soluble in hot water, some substances behave differently. **Cerium(III) sulfate ($Ce_2(SO_4)_3$)** is a rare example where the dissolution process is exothermic. 

Consequently, for this salt, **solubility decreases as temperature increases**.

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you must clearly state the opposing trends:

1.  **$KCl$**: Solubility increases with temperature.
2.  **$Ce_2(SO_4)_3$**: Solubility decreases with temperature.

parts.content: $100\text{ g}$ of $\text{KNO}_3$ is added to $100\text{ g}$ of water at $50^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ that would remain undissolved.
parts.markscheme:
Solubility of $\text{KNO}_3$ at $50^\circ\text{C} = 80\text{ g}/100\text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$100\text{ (g)} - 80\text{ (g)} = 20\text{ (g)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the solubility limit
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Solubility Limit Analysis** approach, we first need to determine the maximum amount of $\text{KNO}_3$ that can dissolve in $100\text{ g}$ of water at $50^\circ\text{C}$. 

Looking at the solubility data for $\text{KNO}_3$ at this temperature:
$$\text{Solubility at } 50^\circ\text{C} = 80\text{ g} \text{ per } 100\text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This means that in $100\text{ g}$ of water, any amount added beyond $80\text{ g}$ will not dissolve and will remain as a solid.

highlights.text: 100\text{ g} of \text{KNO}_3 is added to 100\text{ g} of water at 50^\circ\text{C}

steps.title: Calculate the undissolved mass
steps.content:
Now, we compare the total mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ added to the solubility limit we identified. 

We added $100\text{ g}$ of salt, but the water can only "hold" $80\text{ g}$ in solution. The mass that remains undissolved is the difference between these two values:

$$\text{Undissolved mass} = \text{Total mass added} - \text{Solubility limit}$$
$$\text{Undissolved mass} = 100\text{ g} - 80\text{ g} = 20\text{ g}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for correctly identifying the solubility limit from the graph/table ($80\text{ g}$) and a second mark for the final subtraction.

parts.content: Identify one potential source of error in measuring the solubility and explain its impact on the results.
parts.markscheme:
- Incomplete dissolution of the salt <marks>A1</marks>
- Impact: Leads to an underestimation of solubility values, especially at higher temperatures <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content: In a solubility experiment, we aim to find the maximum amount of salt that can dissolve in 100 mL of water at a specific temperature. A primary source of error is the **incomplete dissolution** of the salt. This occurs if the mixture is not stirred sufficiently or not given enough time to reach a fully saturated state before the measurement is taken.

highlights.text: Identify one potential source of error

steps.title: Explain the resulting impact
steps.content: Because the solution has not reached its maximum capacity (saturation), the recorded mass of the dissolved salt will be less than the actual solubility limit. This leads to an **underestimation** of the solubility values ($S$). This error is often more significant at higher temperatures, where the salt is naturally more soluble and requires more energy and time to fully dissolve.

gdc.instructions: Enter the Temperature values in List 1 and the Solubility values in List 2. Use the 'Graph' function to generate a scatter plot. Notice that the curve steepens as temperature increases.

2. In L1, enter the temperatures: {20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80}.
3. In L2, enter the solubility: {35, 42, 50, 59, 70, 84, 100}.
4. Press [2nd] [STAT PLOT], turn Plot1 On, and select the scatter plot icon.
5. Press [ZOOM] [9] to see the data points.
