steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must identify both the error and its direction:

1.  **Source:** Incomplete dissolution of the salt (1 mark).
2.  **Impact:** Underestimation of solubility values (1 mark).

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content: A common experimental error when measuring solubility at high temperatures is **solvent evaporation**. As the water is heated to reach temperatures like $70^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $80^{\circ}\text{C}$, some of the water molecules gain enough kinetic energy to leave the liquid phase as vapor.

highlights.text: Identify one potential source of error

steps.title: Effect on solvent volume
steps.content: When water evaporates, the actual volume of the solvent in the container decreases. For example, if the student intended to use exactly $100\text{ mL}$ of water, evaporation might reduce the actual volume to $95\text{ mL}$ or less by the time the salt is added.

steps.title: Impact on salt dissolution
steps.content: Solubility is defined as the maximum mass of solute that can dissolve in a specific volume of solvent. Because there is now less water available than recorded, a smaller mass of salt will be required to reach the saturation point compared to what would have dissolved in the full $100\text{ mL}$.

steps.title: Resulting underestimation of solubility
steps.content:
The student calculates the solubility ($S$) using the initial recorded volume (e.g., $100\text{ mL}$), but the mass of salt added was only enough to saturate the smaller, evaporated volume. 

$$\text{Calculated } S = \frac{\text{Mass of salt added}}{\text{Initial recorded volume}}$$

Since the numerator (mass) is lower than it should be for the recorded volume, the final result is an **underestimation** of the actual solubility. This matches the trend noted in the markscheme where errors often lead to lower recorded values.

highlights.text: explain its impact on the results

steps.title: Identify Temperature Placement Error
steps.content: A potential source of error in this investigation is the placement of the thermometer. Specifically, if the temperature is measured in the **water bath** or at the **surface of the liquid** rather than directly within the bulk of the **saturated solution**, the data will be inaccurate.

highlights.text: Identify one potential source of error in measuring the solubility and explain its impact on the results.

steps.title: Explain Thermal Discrepancy
steps.content:
This is an error because of the time required for heat to transfer from the bath to the solution. The recorded temperature will not accurately reflect the state of saturation because the water bath may be at a higher temperature than the solution inside the test tube. 

This relates to **Topic 1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**, where we learn that temperature measurement must represent the average kinetic energy of the specific particles being studied.

steps.title: Impact on Solubility Data
steps.content: The impact of this error is that it leads to **shifted solubility data**. On a solubility curve, the measured solubility values ($S$) will be plotted against temperature values ($T$) that are higher than the actual temperature of the solution, causing a systematic shift in the graph and an incorrect interpretation of the salt's solubility behavior.

parts.content: Suggest one improvement to minimize this error.
parts.markscheme:
- Stir the solution thoroughly and allow sufficient time for dissolution before measuring the solubility <marks>A1</marks>
- Or: Maintain constant temperature using a water bath <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award 2 marks for a detailed valid improvement.</note>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the experimental goal
steps.content: To measure the true **solubility** of a salt at a specific temperature, the solution must be **saturated**. This means it must contain the maximum possible amount of dissolved solute. If the student measures the mass of dissolved salt before the solution has reached this point, the recorded value will be lower than the actual solubility.

steps.title: Stir the solution thoroughly
steps.content: A key way to ensure full saturation is to **stir the solution thoroughly** during the dissolution process. According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (Topic S1.1.2), stirring increases the frequency of collisions between the water molecules and the solid salt, helping the salt to dissolve faster and ensuring that the concentration is uniform throughout the beaker.

steps.title: Allow time for equilibrium
steps.content: Dissolution is a process that approaches a state of **dynamic equilibrium** (Topic R2.3.1). Even with stirring, it takes time for the rate of dissolution to equal the rate of recrystallization. To minimize error, the student should **allow sufficient time** for the salt to reach this equilibrium at each temperature before taking a measurement.

steps.title: Finalized improvement
steps.content:
Combining these ideas provides a robust experimental improvement:

**Stir the solution thoroughly and allow sufficient time for dissolution before measuring the solubility.**

This ensures that the solution is truly saturated, which is essential for accurate solubility data.

steps.title: Identify the source of error
steps.content: Solubility is highly sensitive to temperature. In a standard lab setup, the temperature of the solution can fluctuate or drop quickly while the student is trying to measure the dissolved mass. These fluctuations lead to inconsistent data, especially at higher temperatures like $70^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $80^{\circ}\text{C}$ where the solution cools rapidly to room temperature.

steps.title: Introduce a water bath
steps.content: To minimize this error, you should use a **thermostatically controlled water bath** to maintain the solution at the target temperature. Instead of just heating a beaker, placing the beaker inside a larger volume of water that is kept at a precise temperature ensures better stability.

highlights.text: Maintain constant temperature using a water bath

steps.title: Explain the stabilization process
steps.content: A water bath provides a large thermal mass, which prevents sudden changes in temperature. By allowing the solution to sit in the water bath for several minutes, you ensure that the entire volume reaches a uniform temperature before the solubility is measured. This precision reduces the uncertainty in the independent variable (Temperature, $T$).

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, specify the equipment and why it helps:

1.  **Improvement:** Use a thermostatically controlled water bath.
2.  **Reason:** To maintain a constant and uniform temperature throughout the saturation and measurement process.

This follows the guidance in **Topic 1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**, where we learn that temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy and must be controlled to ensure consistent chemical behavior.

steps.title: Identify the axes
steps.content:
To graph this data, we first need to decide which variable goes on which axis. In an experiment, the variable we change (the independent variable) is usually placed on the $x$-axis, while the variable we measure (the dependent variable) goes on the $y$-axis.

Here, the temperature is controlled, and the solubility is measured at each temperature. Therefore:
- **$x$-axis**: Temperature ($T$)
- **$y$-axis**: Solubility ($S$)

steps.title: Determine the scale
steps.content:
We need to choose a scale that makes the graph easy to read and fills at least half of the provided grid.

- **For Temperature ($T$):** The values range from $20$ to $80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. A scale of $10$ units per major grid square works well.
- **For Solubility ($S$):** The values range from $35$ to $100\,\text{g}/100\,\text{mL}$. Starting the $y$-axis at $0$ and going up to $100$ or $110$ is ideal.

steps.title: Label axes and units
steps.content:
One mark is awarded for correctly labeling the axes with their respective units. In IB Chemistry, we usually use a solidus (slash) to separate the quantity from the unit.

- **$x$-axis label**: Temperature $/ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **$y$-axis label**: Solubility $/ \text{g} \, (100\text{mL})^{-1}$ (or $\text{g} / 100\text{mL}$)

highlights.text: Axes correctly labeled with units (Temperature $/ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and Solubility $/ \text{g} \, (100\text{mL})^{-1}$)

steps.title: Plot the data points
steps.content:
Using a sharp pencil, mark each coordinate pair from the table with a small 'x' or a dot with a circle around it. This ensures precision.

The points to plot are:
$(20, 35), (30, 42), (40, 50), (50, 59), (60, 70), (70, 84), (80, 100)$

steps.title: Draw the best-fit curve
steps.content:
Looking at the points, the solubility does not increase by a constant amount for every $10\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ rise. Because the rate of change is increasing, a straight line would not be appropriate.

Draw a **smooth, single curve** that passes as close as possible to all the points. Avoid 'connecting the dots' with jagged lines (zig-zags).

highlights.text: Correct plotting of all data points and a smooth curve or line of best fit

parts.content: Using the graph, determine the temperature at which a solution containing $60 \text{ g}$ of salt per $100 \text{ mL}$ of water becomes saturated.

steps.title: Identify the target solubility
steps.content: The question asks us to find the temperature when the solubility $S$ is exactly $60 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$. This requires us to use the graph we have constructed from the data points provided in the table.

highlights.text: 60 g of salt per 100 mL of water

steps.title: Locate value on y-axis
steps.content: To find the corresponding temperature, start by locating $60$ on the vertical ($y$) axis, which represents the solubility. From this point, draw a horizontal line across the graph until you reach the smooth curve of best fit.

steps.title: Read corresponding temperature
steps.content:
From the point where your horizontal line meets the curve, draw a vertical line straight down to the horizontal ($x$) axis. The point where this line intersects the axis gives you the temperature.

Looking at our data:
- At $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, solubility is $59 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$.
- At $60^{\circ}\text{C}$, solubility is $70 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$.

Since $60$ is very close to $59$, our temperature should be just above $50^{\circ}\text{C}$.

steps.title: Final answer interpretation
steps.content:
By reading the graph carefully, we find that the temperature is $51^{\circ}\text{C}$. 

In an IB exam, you are awarded one mark for showing the process on your graph (the lines tracing from the axis to the curve) and one mark for the correct numerical value within an acceptable range (usually $\pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$).

steps.title: Identify the target solubility
steps.content: The question asks us to find the temperature for a saturated solution containing $60 \text{ g}$ of salt per $100 \text{ mL}$ of water. We need to look at our data table to find where this value fits.

steps.title: Locate surrounding data points
steps.content:
Looking at the solubility ($S$) column in the table, the value of $60$ falls between the following two points:

*   At $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, solubility is $59 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$
*   At $60^{\circ}\text{C}$, solubility is $70 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$

This tells us the temperature we are looking for is between $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $60^{\circ}\text{C}$.

steps.title: Analyze proximity for estimation
steps.content:
Now, let's see how close $60$ is to our recorded points:

*   Distance from the lower point ($59$): $60 - 59 = 1$
*   Distance from the upper point ($70$): $70 - 60 = 10$

Since $60$ is very close to $59$, the temperature should be only slightly higher than $50^{\circ}\text{C}$.

steps.title: Determine the final temperature
steps.content:
Based on the proximity to the $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ data point, a reasonable numerical estimate is $51^{\circ}\text{C}$. 

The markscheme awards one mark for locating the value on the axis/table and a second mark for providing a value within the range of $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $52^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Final Answer:** $51^{\circ}\text{C}$

highlights.text: Read the corresponding temperature from the horizontal axis: 51^{\circ}\text{C} (Accept 50^{\circ}\text{C} - 52^{\circ}\text{C})

parts.content: Using the graph, estimate the solubility of the salt at $T=55^\circ \mathrm{C}$ using the provided data.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification of $T=55^\circ\text{C}$ on the graph or use of linear interpolation <marks>M1</marks>

- Estimate from graph or calculation: $64.5 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$ (Accept $63 - 66 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$) <marks>A1</marks>

Example calculation (linear interpolation):
$$S=59+\frac{70-59}{60-50} \cdot(55-50)=59+11 \cdot 0.5=64.5 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$$

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify relevant data points
steps.content:
To estimate the solubility at $55^\circ \text{C}$ using a graph, we first look at the data points immediately surrounding our target temperature. In the table, $55^\circ \text{C}$ falls exactly between:

*   $50^\circ \text{C}$ (Solubility = $59 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$)
*   $60^\circ \text{C}$ (Solubility = $70 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$)

steps.title: Plot and draw curve
steps.content:
On a coordinate grid, plot the temperature ($T$) on the horizontal $x$-axis and the solubility ($S$) on the vertical $y$-axis. 

Because the solubility of salts usually increases as temperature rises, you should draw a smooth curve (a line of best fit) that connects your plotted points. This curve allows us to estimate values that weren't directly measured.

steps.title: Locate target temperature
steps.content:
Next, locate $55^\circ \text{C}$ on the $x$-axis. This is the midpoint between the $50$ and $60$ marks. 

Draw a vertical line from $T = 55^\circ \text{C}$ up to where it intersects your smooth curve.

steps.title: Interpolate the solubility
steps.content:
From the intersection point on the curve, draw a horizontal line across to the $y$-axis to read the solubility value ($S$). 

Since $55$ is exactly halfway between $50$ and $60$, the solubility will be approximately halfway between $59$ and $70$. 

$$\text{Solubility} \approx 64.5 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the average of 59 and 70, or use the linear interpolation formula.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the value.

calculator.description: Calculate the exact midpoint (linear interpolation) between the two surrounding data points.

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
The estimated solubility is **$64.5 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$**. 

According to the markscheme, you receive:
*   **1 mark** for correctly identifying $55^\circ \text{C}$ on your graph.
*   **1 mark** for providing an estimate within the acceptable range of **$63 - 66 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$**.

steps.title: Identify bounding data points
steps.content:
To estimate the solubility at $T = 55^\circ\text{C}$ using linear interpolation, we first identify the two temperatures from the table that surround our target value. 

Looking at the table, $55^\circ\text{C}$ falls exactly halfway between $50^\circ\text{C}$ and $60^\circ\text{C}$. We will use the solubility values for these two points:
- At $50^\circ\text{C}$, solubility $S = 59 \text{ g/100 mL}$
- At $60^\circ\text{C}$, solubility $S = 70 \text{ g/100 mL}$

steps.title: Apply linear interpolation formula
steps.content:
Linear interpolation assumes that the change between two points is a straight line. We can find the solubility ($S$) at $55^\circ\text{C}$ by calculating the average of the bounding values (since 55 is the midpoint) or by using the general interpolation formula:

$$S = S_1 + \frac{S_2 - S_1}{T_2 - T_1} \times (T - T_1)$$

Where:
- $T = 55$
- $T_1 = 50, S_1 = 59$
- $T_2 = 60, S_2 = 70$

steps.title: Calculate the estimated solubility
steps.content:
Substituting the values into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} S &= 59 + \frac{70 - 59}{60 - 50} \times (55 - 50) \\ S &= 59 + \frac{11}{10} \times 5 \\ S &= 59 + 1.1 \times 5 \\ S &= 59 + 5.5 \end{aligned}$$

Calculating this gives us $S = 64.5 \text{ g/100 mL}$.

gdc.instructions: Type the expression 59 + (11/10) * 5 into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen: 59 + ((70 - 59) / (60 - 50)) * (55 - 50)

calculator.description: Calculate the solubility using the linear interpolation expression.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The estimated solubility at $55^\circ\text{C}$ is **$64.5 \text{ g/100 mL}$**.

In an IB exam, you earn one mark (**M1**) for showing the interpolation method or identifying the correct temperature range, and one mark (**A1**) for the final value within the acceptable range ($63 - 66 \text{ g/100 mL}$).

steps.title: Identify variables for regression
steps.content: To estimate the solubility at $T = 55^\circ \text{C}$ using regression, we first identify the independent variable ($x = \text{Temperature } T$) and the dependent variable ($y = \text{Solubility } S$). Looking at the data, the solubility increases more rapidly as temperature rises, suggesting an **exponential relationship** rather than a perfectly linear one.

steps.title: Enter data into GDC
steps.content:
Input the given data points into your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). 

| $T$ ($x$) | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ ($y$) | 35 | 42 | 50 | 59 | 70 | 84 | 100 |

Using an exponential regression model ($y = a \cdot b^x$), the calculator determines the best-fit curve for the entire data set.

2. Enter T values in List 1 and S values in List 2.
3. Navigate to [CALC] -> [REG] -> [EXP].

1. Press [STAT] then [EDIT].
2. Enter T values in L1 and S values in L2.
3. Press [STAT], arrow over to [CALC], and select [0:ExpReg].
4. Select L1 for Xlist and L2 for Ylist, then select [Calculate].

desmos.instructions:
1. Create a table with the provided T and S values.
2. In a new cell, type: S_1 ~ a * b^{T_1}

calculator.description: Perform Exponential Regression (ExpReg) to find the model constants.

steps.title: Generate the model equation
steps.content:
From the GDC output, we obtain the approximate values for the exponential model $S = a \cdot b^T$:

$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx 24.80 \\ b &\approx 1.0176 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the regression equation:
$$S = 24.80 \cdot (1.0176)^T$$

steps.title: Calculate solubility at 55°C
steps.content:
Now, substitute the target temperature $T = 55^\circ \text{C}$ into the regression equation to find the estimated solubility $S$:

$$\begin{aligned} S &= 24.80 \cdot (1.0176)^{55} \\ S &\approx 64.6 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL} \end{aligned}$$

This value fits within the acceptable range provided in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 24.80 * (1.0176)^55 in the Run-Matrix menu.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the final result using the exponential model.

steps.title: State the final estimate
steps.content:
Based on the regression analysis, the estimated solubility of the salt at $55^\circ \text{C}$ is approximately **$64.6 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$**.

In an IB exam, you can also use **linear interpolation** between $50^\circ \text{C}$ ($59 \text{ g}$) and $60^\circ \text{C}$ ($70 \text{ g}$) to find a similar value:

$$S = 59 + \frac{70 - 59}{60 - 50} \cdot (55 - 50) = 64.5 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$$

parts.content: The uncertainty in the solubility measurement is $\pm 2 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$. Calculate the percentage uncertainty in the solubility at $T=50^\circ \mathrm{C}$ (where $S=59 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$).
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into percentage uncertainty formula: $\frac{2}{59} \times 100$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Result: $3.4\%$ (Accept $3.39\%$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To calculate the percentage uncertainty, we first need to identify the measured value and its corresponding absolute uncertainty at the specified temperature ($T=50^\circ \text{C}$).

From the question, we have:
- **Measured solubility ($S$):** $59 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$
- **Absolute uncertainty ($\Delta S$):** $\pm 2 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL}$

highlights.text: uncertainty in the solubility measurement is ± 2 g / 100 mL

steps.title: Use percentage uncertainty formula
steps.content:
The percentage uncertainty is a measure of how large the absolute uncertainty is compared to the measured value, expressed as a percentage. While not explicitly in the 'Key Chemistry Equations' list, this is a fundamental skill for all IB Science IA and Paper 3 data analysis tasks.

The formula is:
$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Absolute Uncertainty}}{\text{Measured Value}} \times 100\%$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values we identified in Step 0 into the formula:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{2}{59} \times 100\%$$

Substituting these values correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type '2 / 59 * 100' and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter '2 / 59 * 100' on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type '2 / 59 * 100' into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty by dividing 2 by 59 and multiplying by 100.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives:

$$\frac{2}{59} \times 100 \approx 3.3898...\%$$

Rounding to two significant figures (consistent with the uncertainty value '2'), we get **3.4%**. The markscheme also accepts three significant figures, **3.39%**.

Providing this final result with the correct percentage symbol earns the second mark (**A1**).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 3. Classification of matter > S3.2 Functional groups: Classification of organic compounds > S3.2.9 Infrared spectra (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.9 Infrared spectra (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires interpreting key IR absorption regions (broad 3200–3600 cm⁻¹ for O–H stretch and absence of 1700–1750 cm⁻¹ carbonyl) to identify the most likely organic compound.

options.markscheme: Propane consists only of $$C-H$$ and $$C-C$$ bonds. While it would show absorptions near $$2900 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$, it lacks the $$O-H$$ bond necessary for a broad absorption at $$3200\text{--}3600 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$.
Propanal contains a carbonyl group ($$C=O$$), which produces a strong absorption in the $$1700\text{--}1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$ region, which is explicitly absent in the data provided.

Propan-1-ol contains an alcohol hydroxyl group ($$O-H$$), which produces a characteristic strong, broad absorption in the $$3200\text{--}3600 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$ range. It does not contain a carbonyl group ($$C=O$$), consistent with the absence of a peak at $$1700\text{--}1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$.

Propanoic acid contains a carbonyl group ($$C=O$$), which would produce an absorption at $$1700\text{--}1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$. Additionally, its carboxylic acid $$O-H$$ absorption typically appears in a lower, broader range ($$2500\text{--}3000 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$).

IR spectroscopy identifies functional groups via characteristic bond vibrations, but does not provide the precise relative molecular mass.

TLC separates components and provides $R_f$ values for comparison, but it does not determine relative molecular mass.

parts.content: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$.

steps.title: Identify Cobalt's atomic number
steps.content:
To find the electronic configuration, we first need to know how many electrons a neutral Cobalt atom has. Looking at the Periodic Table in your data booklet, Cobalt is element number 27.

$$Z = 27$$ 

This means a neutral Cobalt atom has 27 protons and 27 electrons.

steps.title: Write the neutral configuration
steps.content:
Following the Aufbau principle (covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**), we fill orbitals in order of increasing energy: $1s \rightarrow 2s \rightarrow 2p \rightarrow 3s \rightarrow 3p \rightarrow 4s \rightarrow 3d$.

For 27 electrons, the configuration is:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^7$$

steps.title: Remove valence electrons
steps.content:
The question asks for the configuration of $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$. A $+2$ charge means the atom has lost two electrons. 

A critical rule for transition metals is that they **lose electrons from the $4s$ orbital before the $3d$ orbital**, even though the $3d$ was filled last. 

Removing the two electrons from the $4s^2$ subshell leaves us with:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The full electronic configuration for $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

In an IB exam, you'll get 1 mark for providing this exact sequence correctly. Note that it is standard to write the $3d$ subshell before or after the $4s$ in the neutral atom, but for the ion, the $4s$ is entirely empty and should not be included in the "full" configuration unless specified.

steps.title: Locate Cobalt
steps.content:
First, we look at the periodic table to find Cobalt ($\text{Co}$). It is located in the first row of the d-block with an atomic number of 27.

This means a neutral cobalt atom has **27 electrons**.

steps.title: Account for the charge
steps.content:
The question specifies cobalt is in a **+2 oxidation state**, meaning we are looking for the configuration of the $\text{Co}^{2+}$ ion. A $+2$ charge means the atom has lost 2 electrons.

$$\text{Total electrons in } \text{Co}^{2+} = 27 - 2 = 25$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

ti84.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

desmos.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

calculator.description: Calculate the number of electrons for the Co2+ ion.

steps.title: Apply transition metal rules
steps.content:
When transition metals in the first d-series (Sc to Zn) form ions, they **lose their $4s$ electrons first**. 

Since we are forming a $+2$ ion, the two $4s$ electrons that would be present in a neutral cobalt atom ($4s^2$) are completely removed. Therefore, the $\text{Co}^{2+}$ ion will have **zero electrons in the $4s$ orbital**.

steps.title: Fill subshells directly
steps.content:
Now we fill the subshells in order until we reach the 3p level, which uses 18 electrons (matching the Argon core):

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$$

To find how many electrons are left for the $3d$ subshell, we subtract these 18 electrons from our total of 25:

$$25 - 18 = 7 \text{ electrons}$$

We place these remaining 7 electrons directly into the $3d$ subshell to get $3d^7$.

gdc.instructions: Subtract 18 from 25.

ti84.instructions: Subtract 18 (the Argon core) from the 25 electrons in the ion.

desmos.instructions: Subtract 18 from 25.

calculator.description: Determine the remaining electrons for the 3d subshell after filling up to 3p.

steps.title: State final configuration
steps.content:
Combining everything together, we list the subshells in order of increasing energy levels. Note that since the $4s$ subshell is empty, it is omitted from the full configuration.

The full electronic configuration is:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

parts.content: Explain why the atomic radius of cobalt is smaller than that of calcium, despite both being in the same period.
parts.markscheme:
- Cobalt has more protons (higher nuclear charge) than calcium <marks>[1]</marks>

- The increased nuclear charge attracts the electron cloud more strongly, pulling the electrons closer to the nucleus and resulting in a smaller atomic radius <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Compare nuclear charges
steps.content:
First, we look at the atomic numbers of both elements on the periodic table. Calcium (Ca) has an atomic number of 20, while Cobalt (Co) has an atomic number of 27. 

Because the atomic number represents the number of protons in the nucleus, cobalt has a significantly higher **nuclear charge** than calcium.

$$\text{Protons in Co} = 27$$
$$\text{Protons in Ca} = 20$$

highlights.text: Cobalt has more protons (higher nuclear charge) than calcium

steps.title: Evaluate shielding and attraction
steps.content:
Both cobalt and calcium are in **Period 4**, which means their valence electrons are being added to the same main energy level ($n=4$). 

Because they are in the same period, the shielding effect from inner-shell electrons remains relatively constant. With shielding held constant, the extra protons in cobalt exert a much stronger electrostatic attraction on the outer electron cloud.

steps.title: Relate attraction to radius
steps.content: The increased nuclear charge in cobalt attracts the electron cloud more strongly. This stronger pull draws the electrons closer to the nucleus, which results in a smaller atomic radius compared to calcium.

highlights.text: The increased nuclear charge attracts the electron cloud more strongly, pulling the electrons closer to the nucleus and resulting in a smaller atomic radius

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of oxygen in CoO.
parts.markscheme:
- Oxidation state of oxygen = $-2$ <marks>[1]</marks>

<note>Accept -II or negative two</note>

steps.title: Identify the sum rule
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Sum of Oxidation States** approach, we use the rule that the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms in a neutral compound must equal zero. This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

We are given that cobalt is in a +2 oxidation state.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Let $x$ be the oxidation state of oxygen. Since the formula for cobalt(II) oxide is $\text{CoO}$, there is one atom of cobalt and one atom of oxygen. We can set up the following algebraic equation:

$$(\text{number of Co} \times \text{ox. state of Co}) + (\text{number of O} \times \text{ox. state of O}) = 0$$

Substituting the known values:
$$(1 \times (+2)) + (1 \times x) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for oxygen
steps.content:
Now, we just need to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 + x &= 0 \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0 - 2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0 - 2 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 0 - 2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Simple subtraction to solve the linear equation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of oxygen in $\text{CoO}$ is $-2$. 

In IB Chemistry, remember that oxidation states are always written with the sign (+ or -) **before** the number.

steps.title: Identify cobalt ion charge
steps.content: From the question specification, we are told that cobalt is in a **+2 oxidation state**. In the context of an ionic compound like cobalt(II) oxide, this means the cobalt exists as a $Co^{2+}$ ion within the lattice.

steps.title: Analyze the formula ratio
steps.content:
The chemical formula provided is $CoO$. This indicates a **1:1 ratio** between cobalt ions and oxygen ions in the crystal lattice. 

This topic is part of **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**, which explores how binary compounds are formed from ions.

steps.title: Apply electrical neutrality
steps.content:
For any ionic compound to be stable, it must be electrically neutral, meaning the sum of all positive and negative charges must equal zero. 

Since we have one $Co^{2+}$ ion, the single oxygen ion must have a charge that cancels out the $+2$ charge:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Charge} &= (\text{Charge of Co}) + (\text{Charge of O}) \\ 0 &= (+2) + x \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

This means the oxygen exists as an oxide ion, $O^{2-}$.

steps.title: State the oxidation state
steps.content:
In simple binary ionic compounds, the oxidation state of an element is equal to the charge of its ion. Since the oxygen ion has a charge of $2-$, its oxidation state is **$-2$**.

According to the markscheme, you can write this as $-2$, -II, or negative two to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
The question specifies that we are looking at **Cobalt(II) oxide (CoO)** and explicitly states that cobalt has an oxidation state of $+2$. 

This is a great starting point because the Roman numeral in the name "Cobalt(II)" directly tells us the oxidation state of the metal ion.

steps.title: Apply oxygen's oxidation rule
steps.content:
According to the standard oxidation rules (covered in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**), oxygen is assigned an oxidation state of $-2$ in most of its compounds, such as oxides.

The only common exceptions to this rule are:
1. **Peroxides** (like $H_2O_2$), where oxygen is $-1$.
2. **Compounds with fluorine** (like $OF_2$), where oxygen can be positive.

Since CoO is a simple metal oxide, we expect oxygen to follow the general rule.

steps.title: Verify using sum rule
steps.content:
In a neutral compound like CoO, the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms must equal zero. We can set up a simple equation to verify our answer:

$$\begin{aligned} (\text{Oxidation state of Co}) + (\text{Oxidation state of O}) &= 0 \\ (+2) + (x) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $x$:
$$x = -2$$

This confirms that the oxidation state of oxygen is $-2$, which is consistent with the $+2$ state of cobalt.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 + (-2) into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the known cobalt state and the calculated oxygen state on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the sum in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the sum of oxidation states is zero.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of oxygen in CoO is **$-2$**.

Note that in Chemistry, we write the sign before the number (e.g., $-2$ or $-II$) for oxidation states, whereas for ionic charges, we typically write the number first (e.g., $2-$).

parts.content: Describe the bonding in cobalt metal, $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$, and explain how it differs from the bonding in CoO.
parts.markscheme:
- Metallic bonding in $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$: lattice of positive ions surrounded by sea of delocalized electrons <marks>[1]</marks>

- Ionic bonding in CoO: $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions held together by electrostatic attraction <marks>[1]</marks>

<note>Accept clear descriptions of the bonding types and differences between them</note>

steps.title: Describe metallic bonding in Co
steps.content:
To understand the bonding in cobalt metal, $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$, we look at its structure as an element. In a metallic lattice, the atoms lose their outer electrons to form a "sea" of mobile electrons. 

Cobalt consists of a **lattice of positive ions (cations)** surrounded by **delocalized electrons**. The bond itself is the strong **electrostatic attraction** between these positive ions and the sea of delocalized electrons.

highlights.text: lattice of positive ions surrounded by sea of delocalized electrons

steps.title: Describe ionic bonding in CoO
steps.content:
In contrast, cobalt(II) oxide ($\mathrm{CoO}$) is a compound formed between a metal and a non-metal. This results in **ionic bonding**. 

In $\mathrm{CoO}$, cobalt exists as $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ cations and oxygen exists as $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ anions. These oppositely charged ions are held together in a 3D lattice by strong **electrostatic attractions** between the **cations and anions**.

highlights.text: Co2+ and O2- ions held together by electrostatic attraction

steps.title: Compare the bonding types
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in what the positive cobalt ions are attracted to:

- In **metallic cobalt**, the attraction is between the $\mathrm{Co}$ cations and **delocalized electrons**.
- In **cobalt(II) oxide**, the attraction is between the $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ cations and **$\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ anions**.

This distinction is covered in **Topic S2: Models of bonding and structure** in your syllabus.

parts.content: Describe the type of bonding in each compound.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the bonding is ionic / electrostatic attraction <marks>M1</marks>
- State that it occurs between oppositely charged ions <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the chemical components
steps.content:
To determine the bonding, we first look at the elements involved in the three compounds: $KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$. 

Each of these substances is a salt composed of a **metal** (Potassium, Sodium, or Magnesium) and **non-metal** elements or groups (Chloride, Carbonate, or Sulfate). This combination typically results in the formation of ions through electron transfer, which is a key characteristic of the **ionic model** covered in **Topic S2.1**.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content:
Since these compounds are formed from metal cations and non-metal anions, the bonding in all three is **ionic**. 

In chemistry terms, this means the primary force holding the particles together is **electrostatic attraction**. Stating either "ionic" or "electrostatic attraction" is necessary to earn the first mark (**M1**) according to the markscheme.

steps.title: Describe the electrostatic attraction
steps.content:
To earn the second mark (**A1**), we must be specific about what is being attracted. An ionic bond is the electrostatic attraction between **oppositely charged ions**.

In these specific cases, the attraction exists between:
- Positive cations: $K^+$, $Na^+$, or $Mg^{2+}$
- Negative anions: $Cl^-$, $CO_3^{2-}$, or $SO_4^{2-}$

steps.title: Finalize the description
steps.content: In summary, the bonding in $KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$ is **ionic**, consisting of the **electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions**.

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, if each compound would conduct electricity in the solid state.
parts.markscheme:
- Conclude that all three compounds will not conduct electricity in the solid state <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that ions are held in fixed positions in the solid state and are not free to move <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the compound type
steps.content:
To answer this, we first need to recognize that all three compounds mentioned—$KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$—are **ionic compounds**. 

They are composed of metal cations ($K^{+}$, $Na^{+}$, $Mg^{2+}$) and non-metal anions or polyatomic ions ($Cl^{-}$, $CO_{3}^{2-}$, $SO_{4}^{2-}$). Knowing the type of bonding is essential because it determines physical properties like electrical conductivity. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Predict the conductivity
steps.content:
Based on our identification, the prediction for all three compounds (X, Y, and Z) is the same: 

**None of the compounds will conduct electricity in the solid state.**

In the IB markscheme, stating that all three do not conduct earns the first mark ($A1$).

highlights.text: conduct electricity in the solid state

steps.title: Explain using lattice theory
steps.content:
The reason lies in the structure of an ionic solid. According to **S2.1.3 Lattice structures and properties**, ionic compounds exist in a giant crystal lattice where ions are held together by strong electrostatic forces. 

In the **solid state**, these ions are held in **fixed positions** within the lattice and are **not free to move**. Since electrical conduction requires the movement of charged particles (mobile ions or delocalized electrons), the solid cannot conduct electricity. 

Providing this explanation earns the second mark ($R1$).

steps.title: Identify Compound X
steps.content:
To determine the conductivity, we first identify the compounds based on the data provided. 

Compound X has a **pH > 7**, which indicates it is basic. Among the options ($KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$), **$Na_2CO_3$** is the only basic salt because the carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) reacts with water to produce $OH^-$ ions. This is confirmed by the observation that a **gas produced** when reacting with $HCl$, which is the characteristic release of carbon dioxide ($CO_2$).

$$Na_2CO_{3(s)} + 2HCl_{(aq)} → 2NaCl_{(aq)} + H_2O_{(l)} + CO_{2(g)}$$ 

Therefore, X is **$Na_2CO_3$**.

steps.title: Identify Compound Y
steps.content:
Compound Y has a **pH of 7**, meaning it is a neutral salt. It shows **no reaction** with either $NaOH$ or $HCl$. This matches the properties of **$KCl$**, which is a salt derived from a strong acid ($HCl$) and a strong base ($KOH$). It does not undergo hydrolysis in water and does not react with dilute $NaOH$ or $HCl$ in a visible way.

Therefore, Y is **$KCl$**.

steps.title: Identify Compound Z
steps.content:
Compound Z also has a **pH of 7**, but unlike Y, a **white precipitate formed** when it reacted with $NaOH$. This is characteristic of the $Mg^{2+}$ ion, which reacts with hydroxide ions to form insoluble magnesium hydroxide:

$$Mg^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2OH^-_{(aq)} → Mg(OH)_{2(s)}$$

This identifies Z as **$MgSO_4$**.

steps.title: Analyze Conductivity
steps.content:
Now that we have identified X, Y, and Z as $Na_2CO_3$, $KCl$, and $MgSO_4$ respectively, we can see that all three are **ionic compounds**.

In the **solid state**, ionic compounds consist of a rigid giant ionic lattice. Because the ions are held in **fixed positions** by strong electrostatic forces of attraction, they are **not free to move** to carry an electric current. 

**Prediction:** None of the compounds (X, Y, or Z) will conduct electricity in the solid state.
**Reason:** The ions are held in fixed positions in the crystal lattice and cannot move.

steps.title: Identify Compound X
steps.content:
To identify Compound X, we look at the observations provided in the table. We see that Compound X has a **pH > 7** and results in **gas produced** when reacted with $HCl$.

In chemistry, salts containing the carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) are basic in aqueous solution and react with acids to produce carbon dioxide gas ($CO_2$). Among our choices, sodium carbonate ($Na_2CO_3$) fits this description perfectly:

$$Na_2CO_3(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow 2NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l) + CO_2(g)$$

Therefore, **Compound X is $Na_2CO_3$**.

steps.title: Identify Compound Z
steps.content:
Next, let's look at Compound Z. The table shows it has a **pH of 7** and a **white precipitate formed** when $NaOH$ is added. 

The question provides a specific hint: "When $NaOH$ is added to a sulfate, a white precipitate is produced." This refers to the formation of magnesium hydroxide, which is insoluble:

$$Mg^{2+}(aq) + 2OH^-(aq) \rightarrow Mg(OH)_2(s)$$

Since $MgSO_4$ contains the $Mg^{2+}$ ion, it will form this white precipitate. Therefore, **Compound Z is $MgSO_4$**.

highlights.text: When $NaOH$ is added to a sulfate, a white precipitate is produced.

steps.title: Identify Compound Y
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate Compound Y. The table indicates a **pH of 7** and **no reaction** with either $NaOH$ or $HCl$.

Potassium chloride ($KCl$) is a salt formed from a strong acid ($HCl$) and a strong base ($KOH$). These types of salts are neutral in water, resulting in a pH of 7. Furthermore, $KCl$ does not react with $NaOH$ to form a precipitate, nor does it react with $HCl$. 

Therefore, **Compound Y is $KCl$**.

steps.title: Analyze the data for X
steps.content:
To identify Compound X, we look for unique characteristics. The table shows that X has a **pH > 7** and results in **gas produced** when reacting with $HCl$.

In our list of compounds ($KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$):
- $Na_2CO_3$ (sodium carbonate) is a salt of a weak acid and a strong base, making it basic ($pH > 7$).
- Carbonates react with acids ($HCl$) to produce carbon dioxide gas ($CO_2$).

Therefore, Compound X must be $Na_2CO_3$.

steps.title: Identify Z using the hint
steps.content:
Next, we use the specific hint provided in the question: "When $NaOH$ is added to a sulfate, a white precipitate is produced." 

Looking at the table for Compound Z, we see the observation **White precipitate formed** when $NaOH$ is added. Since $MgSO_4$ is the only sulfate in our list, and the magnesium ions ($Mg^{2+}$) react with hydroxide ions ($OH^-$) to form insoluble magnesium hydroxide, we can conclude:

Compound Z is $MgSO_4$.

highlights.text: When NaOH is added to a sulfate, a white precipitate is produced.

steps.title: Identify Y by elimination
steps.content:
Now that we have identified X as $Na_2CO_3$ and Z as $MgSO_4$, we can use the **process of elimination** to identify the final compound.

The only substance remaining from our list is $KCl$. We can verify this by checking the table: Compound Y has a **pH of 7** and shows **no reaction** with either $NaOH$ or $HCl$. This matches the properties of $KCl$, which is a neutral salt formed from a strong acid ($HCl$) and a strong base ($KOH$).

Compound Y is $KCl$.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
By matching the observations to the chemical properties of the substances and using the provided hint, we have identified all three compounds:

- **Compound X:** $Na_2CO_3$
- **Compound Y:** $KCl$
- **Compound Z:** $MgSO_4$

parts.content: Explain why $X$ forms an aqueous solution with a pH $> 7$.
parts.markscheme:
- State that $X$ is a basic salt <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that it is produced from a strong base ($NaOH$) and a weak acid ($H_2CO_3$) <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify Compound X
steps.content:
First, we look at the observations for compound **X**. The table shows that when **X** reacts with $HCl$, a **gas is produced**. 

Among the three possible compounds ($KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$), only sodium carbonate ($Na_2CO_3$) reacts with an acid to produce carbon dioxide gas ($CO_2$). Therefore, we can identify **X** as **sodium carbonate** ($Na_2CO_3$).

steps.title: Classify the salt
steps.content:
Since the pH of the aqueous solution of $Na_2CO_3$ is given as **> 7**, we classify it as a **basic salt**. 

In IB Chemistry, the pH of a salt solution depends on the relative strengths of the acid and base used to form it. This is a key concept covered in **Reactivity 3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Analyze parent strengths
steps.content:
To explain why it is basic, we look at its "parent" base and "parent" acid:

1.  The cation, $Na^+$, comes from the parent base **sodium hydroxide** ($NaOH$).
2.  The anion, $CO_3^{2-}$, comes from the parent acid **carbonic acid** ($H_2CO_3$).

Comparing their strengths:
*   $NaOH$ is a **strong base**.
*   $H_2CO_3$ is a **weak acid**.

A salt formed from a **strong base and a weak acid** will result in a basic solution (pH $> 7$).

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must state that it is a basic salt and identify its parents:

*   **Mark 1:** State that $X$ is a **basic salt**.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain it is formed from a **strong base** ($NaOH$) and a **weak acid** ($H_2CO_3$).

steps.title: Identify compound X
steps.content: First, let's identify $X$ based on the data table. The question states that $X$ produces a gas when reacted with $HCl$ and has a pH $> 7$. Among the choices ($KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$), only sodium carbonate ($Na_2CO_3$) reacts with acid to produce carbon dioxide gas and forms a basic solution. This matches the observation in the table.

steps.title: Classify the salt
steps.content:
Sodium carbonate is classified as a **basic salt**. To understand why, we look at the parent acid and base that would form it through neutralization:
- The cation ($Na^+$) comes from a **strong base** ($NaOH$).
- The anion ($CO_3^{2-}$) comes from a **weak acid** ($H_2CO_3$).

When a salt is formed from a strong base and a weak acid, the resulting aqueous solution is basic.

steps.title: Explain salt hydrolysis
steps.content:
The high pH is caused by **salt hydrolysis**. When $Na_2CO_3$ dissolves, the carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) acts as a Brønsted–Lowry base. It accepts a proton from water, creating an equilibrium that produces hydroxide ions ($OH^-$):

$$\begin{aligned} CO_3^{2-}(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons HCO_3^-(aq) + OH^-(aq) \end{aligned}$$

This concept is covered in **Reactivity 3.1.12: The pH of a salt solution**.

steps.title: Relate [OH-] to pH
steps.content:
Because the hydrolysis reaction increases the concentration of hydroxide ions ($OH^-$), the concentration of hydrogen ions ($H^+$) must decrease to maintain the water equilibrium constant:

$$K_{\mathrm{w}}=[\mathrm{H}^{+}][\mathrm{OH}^{-}]$$

A lower $[H^+]$ concentration results in a pH value greater than 7, which explains the basic nature of the solution.

steps.title: Identify Compound Y
steps.content:
To write the correct equation, we first need to identify which of the three compounds ($KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, or $MgSO_4$) corresponds to Compound Y.

Looking at the table, Compound Y has a **pH of 7** and shows **no reaction** with both $NaOH$ and $HCl$. 

*   $Na_2CO_3$ is a basic salt (pH > 7) and reacts with $HCl$ to produce gas ($CO_2$), so it must be X.
*   $MgSO_4$ reacts with $NaOH$ to form a white precipitate of $Mg(OH)_2$, so it must be Z.
*   $KCl$ is a neutral salt (pH 7) formed from a strong acid and a strong base and does not react with $NaOH$ or $HCl$. 

Therefore, **Compound Y is $KCl$ (potassium chloride)**.

steps.title: Select Acid and Base
steps.content:
A neutralization reaction follows the general pattern:
$$\text{acid} + \text{base} \rightarrow \text{salt} + \text{water}$$

Since our salt (Compound Y) is $KCl$, we need to choose an acid that provides the chloride ion ($Cl^-$) and a base that provides the potassium ion ($K^+$):
*   **Acid:** Hydrochloric acid ($HCl$)
*   **Base:** Potassium hydroxide ($KOH$)

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 3.1: Proton transfer reactions**, where you learn about Brønsted–Lowry acids and bases.

steps.title: Write Balanced Equation
steps.content:
Now, we combine the acid and base to form the salt and water. Ensure the equation is balanced with the correct state symbols for a standard aqueous neutralization.

$$HCl(aq) + KOH(aq) \rightarrow KCl(aq) + H_2O(l)$$

In this reaction, the $H^+$ from the acid reacts with the $OH^-$ from the base to form water, leaving the $K^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions in the solution as the salt $KCl$.

parts.content: Identify the gas formed in the reaction of compound $X$ with $HCl$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the gas as carbon dioxide / $CO_2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the pH values
steps.content: To identify compound X, we first look at the provided pH in the table. The student found that an aqueous solution of compound X has a pH higher than 7, which indicates that it is a basic solution.

steps.title: Identify Compound X
steps.content:
We have three candidates: $KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$. 

*   $KCl$ is a neutral salt (pH $\approx$ 7).
*   $MgSO_4$ is typically neutral or slightly acidic in solution.
*   $Na_2CO_3$ (sodium carbonate) contains the carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$), which is a weak base. It reacts with water to produce hydroxide ions ($OH^-$), making the solution basic:

$$CO_3^{2-}(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons HCO_3^-(aq) + OH^-(aq)$$

Since X is the only compound with a pH $>7$, compound X must be $Na_2CO_3$.

steps.title: Identify the gas produced
steps.content:
The table notes that when compound X reacts with $HCl$, a gas is produced. This is a characteristic reaction for carbonates. When a metal carbonate reacts with an acid, it produces a salt, water, and carbon dioxide gas:

$$Na_2CO_3(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow 2NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l) + CO_2(g)$$

Therefore, the gas produced is carbon dioxide ($CO_2$).

steps.title: Analyze the experimental data
steps.content: Looking at the table provided for Compound X, we see a very specific result when it is treated with hydrochloric acid ($HCl$). The observation notes that a gas is produced.

steps.title: Apply acid-carbonate reaction logic
steps.content:
In chemistry, we know that certain functional groups react with acids to release gases. Among the three compounds provided—$KCl$, $Na_2CO_3$, and $MgSO_4$—only the carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce a gas. 

This is a classic neutralization reaction covered in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**. Neither the chloride ion ($Cl^-$) nor the sulfate ion ($SO_4^{2-}$) will produce a gas when reacting with dilute $HCl$.

steps.title: Identify the gas produced
steps.content:
When a metal carbonate reacts with an acid, the products are always a salt, water, and carbon dioxide gas. The chemical equation for the reaction of Compound X ($Na_2CO_3$) with $HCl$ is:

$$Na_2CO_3(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow 2NaCl(aq) + H_2O(l) + CO_2(g)$$

The gas characteristic of this reaction is carbon dioxide.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Based on the reaction of the carbonate ion with acid, the gas formed is carbon dioxide ($CO_2$).

options.content: Magnesium has a higher first ionization energy than sodium, meaning the magnesium cation is more stable than the sodium cation.
options.markscheme: Ionization energy refers to the energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom. While magnesium does have a higher ionization energy, this describes the energy needed to form the ion, not the strength of the electrostatic attraction within the crystal lattice itself.

options.content: The electrostatic forces of attraction are stronger in $$MgO$$ because its ions ($$Mg^{2+}$$ and $$O^{2-}$$) have higher charges than the ions in $$NaCl$$.
options.markscheme: According to Coulomb’s Law, the force of attraction between ions is proportional to the product of their charges. The product of charges in $$MgO$$ ($$2 \times 2 = 4$$) is four times greater than in $$NaCl$$ ($$1 \times 1 = 1$$), resulting in a much stronger ionic bond and a higher melting point.

options.content: The chloride ion has a significantly smaller ionic radius than the oxide ion, which decreases the distance between nuclei in the $$NaCl$$ lattice.
options.markscheme: This statement is factually incorrect. The chloride ion ($$181\text{ pm}$$) is larger than the oxide ion ($$140\text{ pm}$$). A smaller ionic radius actually increases the strength of the ionic bond by allowing the ions to get closer together, which is another reason why $$MgO$$ has a higher melting point than $$NaCl$$.

options.content: Sodium chloride exists as discrete molecular units held together by London dispersion forces, while magnesium oxide forms a giant covalent network.
options.markscheme: Both $$NaCl$$ and $$MgO$$ are ionic compounds that form giant ionic lattices. Neither exists as simple molecules, and neither is a giant covalent structure like diamond or silicon dioxide.

parts.content: Explain how an ionic bond forms between sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) atoms to produce sodium chloride (NaCl). Include the electron transfer process in your explanation.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium (Na) is a metal and has one electron in its outer shell <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Chlorine (Cl) is a nonmetal with seven electrons in its outer shell and needs one more electron to complete its octet <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Sodium transfers one electron to chlorine, resulting in a positively charged sodium ion ($\mathrm{Na}^+$) and a negatively charged chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^-$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- These oppositely charged ions are held together by strong electrostatic forces, forming an ionic bond <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze Sodium's valence electrons
steps.content: First, we look at the position of sodium ($\text{Na}$) in the periodic table. Sodium is a metal in **Group 1**, which means it has **one electron** in its outer valence shell. To reach a stable electron configuration (a full outer shell), it is energetically favorable for sodium to lose this single electron.

highlights.text: Sodium (Na) is a metal and has one electron in its outer shell

steps.title: Analyze Chlorine's valence electrons
steps.content: Next, we look at chlorine ($\text{Cl}$). Chlorine is a non-metal in **Group 17**. It has **seven electrons** in its outer shell. Following the octet rule, chlorine needs **one more electron** to complete its outer shell and become stable.

highlights.text: Chlorine (Cl) is a nonmetal with seven electrons in its outer shell and needs one more electron to complete its octet

steps.title: Describe the electron transfer
steps.content: When these two atoms react, **sodium transfers its one valence electron to chlorine**. This movement allows both atoms to achieve a stable, full outer shell configuration.

highlights.text: Sodium transfers one electron to chlorine

steps.title: Identify the resulting ions
steps.content:
The loss and gain of an electron changes the electrical balance of the atoms:

- Sodium loses an electron (negative charge) to become a **positively charged sodium ion** ($\text{Na}^+$).
- Chlorine gains an electron to become a **negatively charged chloride ion** ($\text{Cl}^-$).

highlights.text: resulting in a positively charged sodium ion (\mathrm{Na}^+) and a negatively charged chloride ion (\mathrm{Cl}^-)

steps.title: Explain the ionic bond
steps.content: Because the $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions have opposite charges, they are drawn together by a **strong electrostatic attraction**. This force of attraction between the oppositely charged ions is what we define as the **ionic bond**.

highlights.text: These oppositely charged ions are held together by strong electrostatic forces, forming an ionic bond

steps.title: Analyze Sodium's electron configuration
steps.content:
To understand how sodium reacts, we first look at its position in the periodic table. Sodium ($\mathrm{Na}$) is an alkali metal in Group 1. It has an atomic number of 11, meaning it has 11 electrons.

Its electron configuration is:
- **Full subshell notation:** $1s^{2} 2s^{2} 2p^{6} 3s^{1}$
- **Simplified shell notation:** 2, 8, 1

Because it has only one electron in its outer (valence) shell, it is energetically favorable for sodium to lose this electron to achieve a stable, full outer shell (an octet) similar to the noble gas Neon.

highlights.text: Sodium (Na) is a metal and has one electron in its outer shell

steps.title: Analyze Chlorine's electron configuration
steps.content:
Next, let's look at Chlorine ($\mathrm{Cl}$), which is a nonmetal in Group 17. It has an atomic number of 17.

Its electron configuration is:
- **Full subshell notation:** $1s^{2} 2s^{2} 2p^{6} 3s^{2} 3p^{5}$
- **Simplified shell notation:** 2, 8, 7

Chlorine has seven electrons in its valence shell. It only needs one more electron to reach a stable octet configuration (the same as Argon).

highlights.text: Chlorine (Cl) is a nonmetal with seven electrons in its outer shell and needs one more electron to complete its octet

steps.title: Explain the electron transfer
steps.content:
When these two atoms react, sodium transfers its single valence electron to chlorine. This transfer allows both atoms to achieve stable noble gas configurations:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Na} (2, 8, 1) &\rightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+} (2, 8) + e^{-} \\ \mathrm{Cl} (2, 8, 7) + e^{-} &\rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{-} (2, 8, 8) \end{aligned}$$

- The sodium atom becomes a **cation** ($\mathrm{Na}^{+}$) because it has lost a negative charge.
- The chlorine atom becomes an **anion** ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$) because it has gained a negative charge.

highlights.text: Sodium transfers one electron to chlorine, resulting in a positively charged sodium ion ($\mathrm{Na}^+$) and a negatively charged chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^-$)

steps.title: Define the ionic bond
steps.content:
An ionic bond is not a physical "link" like a covalent bond; rather, it is the result of physics! 

Because the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ions have opposite charges, they are strongly attracted to one another. This **electrostatic force of attraction** between the oppositely charged ions is what we call an **ionic bond**.

highlights.text: These oppositely charged ions are held together by strong electrostatic forces, forming an ionic bond

parts.content: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$.
parts.markscheme:
$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Start with neutral Chromium
steps.content:
To find the configuration of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we first look at a neutral Chromium atom. Chromium has an atomic number of 24, meaning it has 24 electrons. 

You might expect the configuration to follow the standard filling order ($4s^2 3d^4$), but Chromium is a well-known exception in the IB syllabus due to the stability of half-filled subshells.

steps.title: Identify the Chromium anomaly
steps.content:
The actual electron configuration for neutral $\text{Cr}$ is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^5$$

Instead of $4s^2 3d^4$, one electron from the $4s$ orbital is promoted to the $3d$ orbital. This creates two half-filled subshells ($4s^1$ and $3d^5$), which is a lower energy, more stable state for the atom.

steps.title: Remove electrons for ions
steps.content:
To form the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we must remove three electrons from the neutral atom. 

**Crucial Rule:** For transition metals, electrons are always removed from the **4s subshell before the 3d subshell**, even though the $3d$ subshell was filled last. This is because once the orbitals are occupied, the $4s$ electrons are further from the nucleus and higher in energy.

steps.title: Stepwise electron removal
steps.content:
Let's remove the 3 electrons from $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^5$ one by one:

1.  **Remove 1st electron:** It comes from the $4s$ subshell $\rightarrow 4s^0$.
2.  **Remove 2nd electron:** Since $4s$ is now empty, we take it from the $3d$ subshell $\rightarrow 3d^4$.
3.  **Remove 3rd electron:** We take another from the $3d$ subshell $\rightarrow 3d^3$.

steps.title: State final configuration
steps.content:
After removing those 3 electrons, we are left with the following full electronic configuration for $\text{Cr}^{3+}$:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$$

Note that the $4s^0$ is usually omitted in the final answer as it is empty.

highlights.text: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$.

steps.title: Identify number of electrons
steps.content:
First, we look at the periodic table to find the atomic number of Chromium ($Cr$), which is 24. This means a neutral Chromium atom has 24 protons and 24 electrons.

Since we are looking for the configuration of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we need to account for the $3+$ charge. A positive charge indicates that electrons have been lost.

highlights.text: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$

steps.title: Calculate the electron count
steps.content:
To find the total number of electrons in the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we subtract the charge from the atomic number:

$$\text{Electrons} = 24 - 3 = 21$$

We need to distribute these 21 electrons into the subshells.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the charge (3) from the atomic number of Chromium (24).

ti84.instructions: Enter the atomic number minus the charge.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the total electrons by subtracting the charge from the atomic number.

calculator.description: Verify the number of electrons for Cr3+.

steps.title: Fill subshells in order
steps.content:
Using the Direct Subshell Filling approach for transition metal ions, we fill the orbitals in order of increasing energy. A key rule for transition metal ions is that the $4s$ subshell is higher in energy than the $3d$ subshell, so the $4s$ remains empty and we fill $3d$ immediately after $3p$.

Assign the 21 electrons following the maximum capacity of each subshell ($s=2, p=6, d=10$):

1.  **1s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 1s^2$ (Total: 2)
2.  **2s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 2s^2$ (Total: 4)
3.  **2p**: 6 electrons $\rightarrow 2p^6$ (Total: 10)
4.  **3s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 3s^2$ (Total: 12)
5.  **3p**: 6 electrons $\rightarrow 3p^6$ (Total: 18)

We have $21 - 18 = 3$ electrons remaining.

steps.title: Assign remaining electrons
steps.content:
The remaining 3 electrons are placed directly into the $3d$ subshell because, in transition metal ions, the $4s$ subshell is not occupied.

$$3d^3$$

This brings our total count to 21 electrons.

gdc.instructions: Add up the electrons in each subshell to verify the total.

ti84.instructions: Sum the exponents of the configuration.

desmos.instructions: Verify the sum of electrons.

calculator.description: Sum the electrons to ensure they equal 21.

steps.title: State the final configuration
steps.content:
Combining all the subshells in order, we get the full electronic configuration for $\text{Cr}^{3+}$. 

This matches the requirement for the **1 mark** available in the markscheme:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$$

parts.content: Explain why chromium shows variable oxidation states, while calcium does not.
parts.markscheme:
Chromium has electrons in both $3d$ and $4s$ subshells which are close in energy, allowing it to lose varying numbers of electrons <marks>M1</marks>

Calcium only has electrons in the $4s$ subshell available for bonding and has a large increase in ionization energy after these are removed / limits it to a $+2$ oxidation state <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify electron configurations
steps.content:
To understand oxidation states, we first look at the electron configurations of both elements. These configurations show which electrons are available for bonding.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ca: } & [Ar] 4s^2 \\ \text{Cr: } & [Ar] 3d^5 4s^1 \end{aligned}$$

This highlights the different subshells that might be involved in losing electrons.

steps.title: Analyze chromium's energy proximity
steps.content: In chromium, the **$4s$ and $3d$ subshells are very close in energy**. Because the energy difference (proximity) between these subshells is small, electrons from both can be lost or shared during chemical reactions. This allows chromium to achieve various stable oxidation states, such as $+2, +3,$ and $+6$.

steps.title: Analyze calcium's energy gap
steps.content:
For calcium, only the two electrons in the **$4s$ subshell** are valence electrons. The next available electrons are in the $3p$ subshell, which is a stable core level significantly lower in energy. 

There is a **large energy gap** between the $4s$ and $3p$ subshells. This leads to a massive increase in ionization energy after the first two electrons are removed, effectively limiting calcium to a single $+2$ oxidation state.

steps.title: Summarize the key difference
steps.content:
The key reason for the difference is the energy relationship between subshells:

*   **Chromium:** The small energy gap between $3d$ and $4s$ allows for flexibility in the number of electrons lost.
*   **Calcium:** The large energy gap between $4s$ and the core $3p$ electrons prevents the loss of more than two electrons.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S3.1.9: Oxidation states of transition elements**.

steps.title: Look at electron configurations
steps.content:
To understand ionization energies, we first look at the electron configurations of both elements:

*   **Calcium ($Z=20$):** $[Ar] 4s^2$
*   **Chromium ($Z=24$):** $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$

This shows us which electrons are in the outer subshells and available for removal during ionization.

steps.title: Analyze Calcium's ionization energy
steps.content:
Calcium has two electrons in its $4s$ subshell. Removing these two electrons forms the stable $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ion, which has a noble gas configuration ($[Ar]$).

If we try to remove a third electron, it must come from the stable $3p$ subshell. This requires a massive amount of energy because the electron is closer to the nucleus and less shielded. In a graph of successive ionization energies, we see a **large jump** after the second electron is removed, which limits calcium to the $+2$ oxidation state.

highlights.text: large increase in ionization energy after these are removed / limits it to a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Analyze Chromium's ionization energy
steps.content:
Chromium is a transition metal. Its $4s$ and $3d$ subshells are very **close in energy**. 

Because these levels are so similar, the successive ionization energies for chromium increase relatively gradually as electrons are removed from both the $4s$ and the $3d$ subshells. There is no immediate massive energy jump after the first or second electron is lost. This allows chromium to lose different numbers of electrons (e.g., $+2, +3, +6$) depending on the chemical environment.

highlights.text: Chromium has electrons in both 3d and 4s subshells which are close in energy, allowing it to lose varying numbers of electrons

steps.title: Synthesize the final answer
steps.content:
In summary, the key difference lies in the energy gap:

1.  **Chromium** can show variable oxidation states because the energy difference between the $4s$ and $3d$ subshells is small, allowing for a gradual increase in successive ionization energies.
2.  **Calcium** is restricted to $+2$ because removing a third electron involves a massive jump in ionization energy as it breaks into a stable noble gas core.

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of chromium in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.
parts.markscheme:
Let oxidation state of Cr be $x$
Oxidation state of O = $-2$
$2x + 3(-2) = 0$
$2x = 6$
$x = +3$
Oxidation state of Cr = $+3$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award mark for correct answer with or without working.</note>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the compound type
steps.content: To find the oxidation state of chromium in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, we first recognize that this is a neutral compound. According to the rules of oxidation states (which you can find in **Topic S3.1.6 Oxidation states**), the sum of all oxidation states in a neutral molecule must be equal to zero.

steps.title: Assign known oxidation states
steps.content:
Oxygen almost always has an oxidation state of $-2$ when it is in a compound (except in peroxides or when bonded to fluorine). 

Let's define $x$ as the oxidation state of a single chromium atom.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We have two chromium atoms and three oxygen atoms in the formula $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. Using the algebraic summation method, we set the total sum to zero:

$$2(x) + 3(-2) = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$2x - 6 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for chromium
steps.content:
Now, we just need to isolate $x$ using basic algebra:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x &= +6 \\ x &= \frac{+6}{2} \\ x &= +3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 6 divided by 2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 6 divided by 2.

desmos.instructions: Enter 6 divided by 2.

calculator.description: Verify the division to find the oxidation state per chromium atom.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of chromium is **$+3$**. 

In IB Chemistry, always remember to include the sign ($+$ or $-$) before the number when writing oxidation states! This distinguishes them from ion charges (like $3+$).

highlights.text: Calculate the oxidation state of chromium

steps.title: Identify the formula subscripts
steps.content:
To use the **Ionic Deduction Method**, we start by looking at the chemical formula provided: $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. 

In this formula, the subscripts tell us the ratio of atoms:
- There are **2** Chromium (Cr) atoms.
- There are **3** Oxygen (O) atoms.

This method is related to the **Ionic Model** covered in **Topic S2.1**, where we learn how binary compounds are formed from ions.

steps.title: Reverse the cross-over rule
steps.content:
When forming an ionic compound, we often use the 'cross-over rule' where the charge of one ion becomes the subscript of the other. To find the oxidation state, we simply reverse this process:

1. Move the subscript of Oxygen (**3**) up to become the charge of Chromium.
2. Move the subscript of Chromium (**2**) up to become the charge of Oxygen.

$$\text{Cr}_2 \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} \text{ and } \text{O}^{2-}$$

steps.title: Determine the oxidation state
steps.content:
From our reverse cross-over, we see that the Chromium ion has a charge of $+3$. 

In a simple ionic model, the oxidation state is equal to the charge of the ion. Since Chromium is a metal and Oxygen is a non-metal, the Chromium will have the positive value.

- Charge on $\text{Cr}$ = $+3$
- Charge on $\text{O}$ = $-2$

This is consistent with Oxygen's standard oxidation state of $-2$.

steps.title: Verify the total charge
steps.content:
We can quickly verify that the sum of oxidation states in the neutral compound $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ equals zero:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(\text{Cr}) + 3(\text{O}) &= 0 \\ 2(+3) + 3(-2) &= 6 - 6 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

The oxidation state of chromium is **+3**.

desmos.instructions: Input the expression to verify the sum.

calculator.description: Verify that the sum of oxidation states for Cr (+3) and O (-2) equals zero.

parts.content: Describe the bonding in chromium metal, $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{s})$, and compare it to the bonding in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.
parts.markscheme:
In chromium metal, bonding is metallic, involving a lattice of positive ions/cations surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons <marks>A1</marks>

In $\mathrm{Cr}_2\mathrm{O}_3$, bonding is ionic, involving the electrostatic attraction between $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ ions and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify substance types
steps.content:
To compare the bonding, we first identify the type of substance for each. 

*   **Chromium ($\text{Cr}$)** is a transition metal, so it will exhibit **metallic bonding**.
*   **Chromium(III) oxide ($\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$)** is a compound formed between a metal (chromium) and a non-metal (oxygen), which typically results in **ionic bonding**.

You can review these models in **Topic S2: Models of bonding and structure**.

steps.title: Describe metallic bonding
steps.content:
In solid chromium metal, the atoms lose their outer electrons to form a regular lattice of **positive ions (cations)**. These electrons are no longer associated with a single nucleus and are free to move throughout the structure. 

The bonding is the **electrostatic attraction** between this lattice of positive ions and the **"sea" of delocalized electrons**.

steps.title: Describe ionic bonding
steps.content:
In chromium(III) oxide, electrons are transferred from chromium to oxygen. This creates $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. 

The bonding here is **ionic**, which consists of the **electrostatic attraction** between these **oppositely charged ions** arranged in a giant 3D lattice.

steps.title: Summary of comparison
steps.content:
To get full marks, ensure you mention the specific particles and the forces involved for both:

1.  **$\text{Cr}$:** Metallic bonding; attraction between a lattice of $\text{Cr}$ cations and delocalized electrons.
2.  **$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$:** Ionic bonding; attraction between $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions.
